stem: A 7-week-old former 38-week infant presents to a metropolitan paediatric emergency department in winter with 4 days of coryza and 24 hours of worsening work of breathing. He has been refusing feeds for 8 hours and has had 3 short apnoeas witnessed in triage. He is on standard nasal-prong oxygen at 1 L/min. RSV PCR is positive. He has had no fever above 38.0 degrees C and chest X-ray shows hyperinflation without focal consolidation. The treating registrar has commenced him on 2 L/kg/min heated humidified high-flow nasal cannula with FiO2 0.4 thirty minutes ago.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 178, RR 78, SpO2 89% on HFNC FiO2 0.4, temperature 37.6 degrees C, CRT 3 seconds
data_table.Examination: Marked subcostal and intercostal recession, head bobbing, fine crackles bilaterally, mottled peripheries
data_table.Investigations: VBG: pH 7.24, pCO2 64 mmHg, HCO3 26, lactate 2.1 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue current HFNC settings and reassess in 30 minutes
options.rationale: Persistent hypercapnia, falling saturations and recurrent apnoeas on established HFNC define HFNC failure; further passive observation risks respiratory arrest. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Trial nebulised adrenaline 5 mg with reassessment at 15 minutes
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline has not been shown to alter the natural history of bronchiolitis and does not address the apnoeas or hypercapnia driving deterioration here. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis; PREDICT bronchiolitis guideline)

options.text: Order an urgent CT chest and consult paediatric infectious diseases
options.rationale: Imaging beyond the existing chest X-ray will not change acute management; the priority is ventilatory support, and apnoeas in RSV infants are characteristic and do not need ID review. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Administer IV salbutamol 5 mcg/kg over 10 minutes and reassess
options.rationale: Beta-2 agonists are not recommended in bronchiolitis under 12 months; salbutamol risks tachycardia and lactataemia without bronchodilator response in this age group. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Escalate to non-invasive ventilation with PEEP 6 cmH2O and contact PICU
options.rationale: HFNC failure (rising pCO2, apnoeas, persistent saturations under 90%) in an infant under 3 months meets escalation criteria; NIV with PEEP recruits collapsed alveoli and PICU transfer secures airway support. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis; ANZICS paediatric NIV)

explanation.summary: Bronchiolitis escalation in Australian paediatric units follows a stepwise model: minimal handling and nasogastric or IV fluids, supplemental oxygen, HFNC for SpO2 under 92 percent or moderate work of breathing, then NIV or intubation for HFNC failure. HFNC failure is defined by persistent SpO2 under 90 percent at FiO2 above 0.4, rising pCO2 above 60 mmHg, or recurrent apnoeas. NIV with PEEP 5 to 7 cmH2O is the next step before intubation in most centres. PICU contact is mandatory at the point of HFNC failure.
key_points: HFNC failure criteria: FiO2 over 0.4 with SpO2 under 90 percent, pCO2 over 60 mmHg, or apnoeas
Salbutamol and adrenaline are not recommended in infants under 12 months with bronchiolitis
Apnoeas are typical of RSV in infants under 3 months and warrant high-acuity observation
Escalate to NIV before intubation when criteria met; involve PICU early
explanation.pearls: In an infant under 3 months on HFNC at 2 L/kg/min with FiO2 above 0.4 and any apnoea within 30 minutes, escalate to NIV that hour - do not retry HFNC settings.

subtopic_detail: Hand foot and mouth disease counselling
stem: You are the GP seeing a 3-year-old girl whose mother is worried she has chickenpox. Two days ago the child developed a low-grade fever and refused breakfast. This morning small grey-white blisters appeared on her palms, soles and the inside of her cheeks. Her older brother attends the same childcare centre. The family is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander and the girl is fully immunised including two doses of varicella vaccine. Mum asks when she can go back to childcare.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6°C, HR 110, RR 24, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Pinpoint vesicles on a non-erythematous base on hands, feet and buccal mucosa; trunk and face spared; alert and drinking small volumes
data_table.Investigations: Not indicated

lead_in: What is the most appropriate advice regarding return to childcare?

options.text: Intramuscular adrenaline 10 micrograms/kg into the lateral thigh and observe response over 5 minutes
options.rationale: The Staying Healthy 5th edition advises exclusion of children with HFMD until the blisters have dried, with the child also clinically well; this aligns the duration to actual infectivity and clinical state rather than a fixed day count. (Source: Staying Healthy 5th edition NHMRC)

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg infused over 20 minutes with cardiac monitoring in HDU
options.rationale: HFMD is not on the standard national notifiable list and is managed by exclusion plus hygiene; PHU notification is only triggered in clusters of severe disease such as suspected EV71-associated brainstem encephalitis. (Source: CDNA Series of National Guidelines)

options.text: IV aminophylline 10 mg/kg load over 60 minutes on the general paediatric ward with telemetry only
options.rationale: HFMD does carry an exclusion period in Australian early childhood settings; absence of fever does not change the recommendation while the vesicles are still moist. (Source: Staying Healthy 5th edition NHMRC)

options.text: Switch to high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min FiO2 0.5 and reassess at 30 minutes off bronchodilator
options.rationale: There is no mandated 21 day exclusion for HFMD; this length applies to no notifiable childhood infection currently listed in the NHMRC schedule. (Source: Staying Healthy 5th edition NHMRC)

options.text: Switch corticosteroid route to IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg slow bolus and continue current nebulisers
options.rationale: This is the rule for varicella, not for hand foot and mouth disease; the vesicles in HFMD do not crust like chickenpox and prolonged exclusion is not required. (Source: Staying Healthy: Preventing infectious diseases in early childhood education and care services)

explanation.summary: Hand foot and mouth disease is a common coxsackievirus or enterovirus rash with grey-white vesicles on the palms, soles and oral mucosa. The Staying Healthy 5th edition recommends exclusion until the blisters have dried; there is no fixed day count and crusting is not part of the natural history. Hygiene measures and surface cleaning at the childcare centre are the main control strategies, with EV71 surveillance flagged separately for severe disease.
key_points: Exclude from childcare until blisters have dried, not until they have crusted.
There is no fixed day count for return; clinical wellness is required in addition.
Notification is only triggered in clusters with severe neurological involvement suggesting EV71.
explanation.pearls: If a child with HFMD develops ataxia, drowsiness or myoclonic jerks within the first 5 days, escalate urgently because enterovirus 71 brainstem encephalitis is a public health priority.

stem:
A 6-year-old boy (weight 22 kg) with known peanut allergy is brought to a tertiary ED by ambulance 25 minutes after biting a satay skewer at a family function. Within minutes he developed urticaria, lip swelling, vomiting, and audible stridor. He received IM adrenaline 300 microg into the lateral thigh by his parents at the scene and a second IM adrenaline 300 microg by paramedics 6 minutes later, with two boluses of 0.9% sodium chloride 20 mL/kg en route.

On arrival he is given high-flow oxygen 15 L/min via non-rebreather and a nebulised adrenaline 5 mg has just been started for persistent stridor. He has had no previous anaphylaxis admission and is not on beta-blockers. Despite these measures he remains hypotensive with ongoing wheeze, persistent stridor, and a flushed mottled appearance ten minutes after the second IM dose. The PICU registrar is en route.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 158 bpm, RR 38 /min, SpO2 91% on 15 L/min non-rebreather, BP 72/40 mmHg, T 36.8 deg C
data_table.Examination: Diffuse urticaria, marked lip and periorbital oedema, biphasic stridor, widespread expiratory wheeze, capillary refill 4 seconds, GCS 14 (drowsy but rousable)
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.29, lactate 4.4 mmol/L, K 3.6 mmol/L, glucose 6.1 mmol/L; tryptase taken; ECG sinus tachycardia
data_table.Medications received: IM adrenaline 300 microg x2, 0.9% saline 20 mL/kg x2, nebulised adrenaline 5 mg in progress, high-flow oxygen
data_table.History: Peanut allergy with prior urticaria only, no asthma, no beta-blocker use, fully immunised

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Administer IV hydrocortisone and IV chlorphenamine 2.5 mg, then reassess at 15 minutes
options.rationale: Steroids and antihistamines do not treat airway/CV collapse and have no role as the next escalation after adrenaline failure. Would be correct only as adjunct after definitive adrenaline (Source: ASCIA).
source_refs: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines

options.text: Third IM adrenaline 300 microg into lateral thigh, repeat every 5 minutes
options.rationale: After two or more IM doses without response, refractory anaphylaxis is declared; IV infusion is preferred for sustained titratable effect. Would be correct only if IV access were unavailable (Source: ASCIA).
source_refs: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines

options.text: Commence an IV adrenaline infusion titrated to BP, with continuous cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: Refractory anaphylaxis (persistent hypotension or airway compromise after at least two IM doses) is treated with a titrated IV adrenaline infusion starting at 0.1 microg/kg/min in a monitored area, with PICU involvement, as per ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines and RCH CPG: Anaphylaxis.
source_refs: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines

options.text: Give IV glucagon as a bolus to overcome adrenaline-resistant shock from beta-blockade
options.rationale: Glucagon is for refractory anaphylaxis in beta-blocked patients; this child is not on beta-blockers. Would be correct only with documented beta-blocker use (Source: ASCIA).
source_refs: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines

options.text: Give IV hydroxocobalamin for suspected concurrent cyanide-mediated vasodilation today
options.rationale: Hydroxocobalamin is the cyanide-toxicity antidote (smoke inhalation), no role in anaphylaxis. Would be correct only with smoke inhalation and suspected cyanide toxicity (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).

explanation.summary: Refractory anaphylaxis is failure to respond to two or more IM adrenaline doses. Management is an IV adrenaline infusion in a monitored area (PICU/ED resus) alongside ongoing fluid resuscitation, oxygen, and airway preparation. Steroids and antihistamines are adjuncts, not next steps. Glucagon is reserved for beta-blocked patients.
key_points: Refractory anaphylaxis = persistent hypotension or airway compromise after >=2 IM adrenaline doses
Start IV adrenaline infusion at 0.1 microg/kg/min and titrate
Continuous cardiac monitoring is mandatory
Fluid resuscitation 20 mL/kg crystalloid boluses repeated as needed
Tryptase taken acutely and at 1-2 hours and at 24 hours
explanation.pearls: Stridor in anaphylaxis is laryngeal oedema, not bronchospasm; nebulised adrenaline buys time but does not replace IV adrenaline.

sources.label: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines

stem:
A 9-year-old boy with severe persistent asthma (one PICU admission 18 months ago for life-threatening wheeze, baseline fluticasone-salmeterol BD via spacer, salbutamol PRN) is brought to a tertiary ED after 12 hours of progressive wheeze triggered by a viral URTI in the family. In ED over the past 60 minutes he has received continuous back-to-back nebulised salbutamol 5 mg with ipratropium 250 microg every 20 minutes for three doses, oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg with antiemetic cover, and high-flow oxygen at 10 L/min via nebuliser mask.

There has been no clinical improvement: he remains exhausted, leaning forward, speaking only in single words, and looks visibly tired between breaths. He has not yet received any IV bronchodilators, IV magnesium, or aminophylline. He is normotensive but markedly tachycardic with a fine tremor and palpitations from the salbutamol. There has been no fever, no foreign body history, and no preceding anaphylactic features. The PICU outreach team has reviewed at the bedside and recommends escalation but has not yet started any infusions while awaiting the ED decision.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 158 bpm, RR 42 /min, SpO2 88% on 10 L/min via nebuliser mask, BP 116/72 mmHg, T 37.4 deg C
data_table.Examination: Tripoding, single-word speech, marked accessory muscle use, silent chest in upper zones, faint expiratory wheeze in lower zones, GCS 15 but visibly exhausted, fine tremor
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.30 pCO2 50 mmHg HCO3 22 mmol/L lactate 3.8 mmol/L, K 3.0 mmol/L, glucose 9.4 mmol/L; CXR hyperinflation, no pneumothorax
data_table.Medications: Continuous nebulised salbutamol/ipratropium x3 doses, oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg given 35 min ago, no IV bronchodilator yet
data_table.History: Previous PICU admission age 7.5, fluticasone-salmeterol BD baseline, no LABA-only, no NSAID use

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue back-to-back nebulised salbutamol 5 mg every 20 minutes and reassess in 1 hour
options.rationale: Already failed three back-to-back nebulised doses plus systemic steroid; persisting risks decompensation. Would be correct only at an earlier step before escalation (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Asthma).

options.text: IV magnesium sulfate (max 2 g) over 20 minutes with cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: Severe acute asthma failing oxygen, back-to-back salbutamol/ipratropium and systemic steroid escalates to IV magnesium sulfate before IV salbutamol or aminophylline, as per RCH CPG: Asthma acute and Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory.

options.text: IV aminophylline loading over 60 minutes followed by maintenance infusion
options.rationale: Aminophylline is reserved for failure of IV magnesium and IV salbutamol due to narrow therapeutic index and arrhythmia risk. Would be correct as third-line in PICU (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: IV salbutamol bolus over 10 minutes followed by infusion
options.rationale: IV salbutamol typically follows IV magnesium; potassium 3.0 and lactate 3.8 will worsen with more beta-agonist. Would be correct after IV magnesium failure (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Immediate rapid sequence intubation with ketamine and rocuronium
options.rationale: Intubation in severe asthma risks bronchospasm, hypotension and barotrauma; reserved for impending arrest after maximal medical therapy. Would be correct only after maximal therapy and impending arrest (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Severe paediatric asthma is identified by tachypnoea, accessory muscle use, oxygen saturation under 92%, inability to complete sentences, agitation or drowsiness, and a silent chest (impending arrest). Stepwise Australian management is oxygen to maintain SpO2 over 94%, back-to-back nebulised salbutamol 5 mg with ipratropium 250 micrograms every 20 minutes for the first hour, systemic corticosteroid (oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg or IV methylprednisolone 1 mg/kg), then IV magnesium sulfate 25-50 mg/kg over 20 minutes if no improvement. Further escalation is IV salbutamol bolus then infusion, then IV aminophylline (rarely used now), with PICU transfer alongside. Intubation is the last resort: hypoxia and hypercapnia rise quickly post-intubation due to high airway pressures, so ketamine is the induction agent of choice for its bronchodilator effect. Permissive hypercapnia and lung-protective ventilation are the rule.
key_points: IV magnesium dose: 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) over 20 min
Silent chest indicates life-threatening asthma
Watch for hypokalaemia and lactic acidosis from salbutamol
Aminophylline is last-line because of arrhythmia risk
Involve PICU early when failing nebs + steroid
explanation.pearls: IV magnesium sulfate 25-50 mg/kg (max 2 g) over 20 minutes is the standard third-line agent in severe paediatric asthma not responding to back-to-back salbutamol/ipratropium plus oral corticosteroid - works on bronchial smooth muscle Ca2+ channels (RCH CPG Asthma).

stem:
A 14-month-old boy (weight 11 kg) presents to a regional ED with 12 hours of barking cough, hoarse cry and worsening stridor at rest. He received nebulised adrenaline 5 mg (1:1000) and oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg 45 minutes ago. He had transient improvement for 20 minutes but is now again stridulous at rest with worsening recessions, increasing work of breathing, intermittent quiet episodes followed by paradoxical chest movement.

He is restless and difficult to console, and SpO2 is dropping. There is no drooling, no fever above 38.0 deg C and no foreign body history. He is fully immunised. The anaesthetic and ENT registrars have been paged and are 5 minutes away. The team is preparing the difficult-airway trolley. Current observations: HR 168, RR 48, SpO2 88 percent on 8 L/min Hudson mask, BP 102/66, T 37.6 deg C, with marked biphasic stridor, severe recessions, intermittent paradoxical chest wall movement and circumoral cyanosis.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168 bpm, RR 48 /min, SpO2 88% on 8 L/min Hudson mask, BP 102/66 mmHg, T 37.6 deg C
data_table.Examination: Marked biphasic stridor, severe sternal and subcostal recessions, intermittent paradoxical chest wall movement, restless and not consolable, cyanosis around the lips
data_table.Treatment given: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mg x1 (45 min ago), oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg (45 min ago)
data_table.History: Barking cough 12 h, no drooling, no high fever, fully immunised, no FB history

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Wait 30 minutes for dexamethasone to take full effect before any further intervention
options.rationale: Impending respiratory failure (hypoxia, exhaustion, paradoxical breathing); passive waiting is unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Croup).

options.text: Repeat nebulised adrenaline 5 mg and continue ward observation; defer airway team involvement
options.rationale: Repeat adrenaline is reasonable as a bridge but does not substitute for definitive airway when impending failure is present. Would be correct only as bridge alongside airway team activation (Source: RCH CPG: Croup).

options.text: Commence high-flow nasal cannula oxygen 2 L/kg/min and reassess at 30 minutes without airway team involvement
options.rationale: HFNC does not bypass critical subglottic narrowing and must not delay airway team. Would be correct only in milder distress without impending failure (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Repeat nebulised adrenaline as a bridge while transferring with the airway team to theatre for gas inhalational induction by anaesthetics, with ENT present and a smaller-than-predicted ETT prepared
options.rationale: Severe croup unresponsive to initial adrenaline plus steroid with signs of impending respiratory failure requires definitive airway management in theatre under inhalational induction (preserving spontaneous ventilation), with ENT standby for surgical airway and a smaller-than-predicted ETT. Repeat adrenaline acts as a bridge during transfer.

options.text: Rapid sequence intubation in the ED with rocuronium and an age-appropriate cuffed ETT
options.rationale: RSI with paralysis risks losing the airway when bag-mask ventilation may be difficult; inhalational induction preserving spontaneous ventilation in theatre is standard. Would be correct only in catastrophic airway loss when inhalational induction impossible (Source: ANZCA airway guidance).

explanation.summary: Severe croup unresponsive to nebulised adrenaline and dexamethasone with signs of impending respiratory failure (hypoxia, exhaustion, decreasing air entry, paradoxical breathing) requires definitive airway in theatre. Inhalational induction with preserved spontaneous ventilation is preferred over paralysis-based RSI. ENT must be on standby for a potential surgical airway, and a smaller-than-predicted ETT should be selected.
key_points: Repeat nebulised adrenaline every 20-30 minutes as needed as a bridge
Definitive airway in theatre when impending failure
Inhalational induction; avoid paralysis
Use ETT one size smaller than predicted
ENT for emergency surgical airway
explanation.pearls: Quiet stridor with exhaustion is more ominous than loud stridor with vigorous breathing.

subtopic_detail: Duct-dependent cyanotic neonatal cardiac lesion presentation
stem: You are the neonatal registrar called to the postnatal ward at hour 36 of life. A term male is increasingly cyanosed and lethargic. He passed initial pulse oximetry screening. He now has central cyanosis, weak pulses in all four limbs and a hyperdynamic precordium without murmur. Pre-ductal SpO2 is 65% and post-ductal SpO2 is 64% in air, unchanged on 100% oxygen for 10 minutes. Capillary blood gas shows pH 7.18, lactate 7.2 mmol/L, base excess -12, normal glucose.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.4 °C, HR 178, RR 70, SpO2 65% pre-ductal, 64% post-ductal, BP 56/34 mean 41
data_table.Examination: Central cyanosis, weak pulses all four limbs, no murmur, hepatomegaly 2 cm; no respiratory distress
data_table.Investigations: ECG normal QRS axis; CXR mildly enlarged cardiac silhouette, oligaemic lung fields

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence intravenous prostaglandin E1 infusion at 10 nanograms/kg/min and call cardiology
options.rationale: Failed hyperoxia with cyanosis in the first 48 hours indicates a duct-dependent cyanotic lesion; prostaglandin E1 maintains ductal patency until definitive diagnosis and surgery. (Source: RCH CPG Cyanotic neonate)

options.text: Increase FiO2 to 100% by nasal high flow and admit to special care nursery for monitoring
options.rationale: Persistent cyanosis on 100% FiO2 over 10 minutes is the failed hyperoxia test diagnostic of cyanotic congenital heart disease; oxygen alone will not address the lesion. (Source: RCH CPG Cyanotic neonate)

options.text: Treat for presumed early-onset sepsis with intravenous benzylpenicillin and gentamicin only
options.rationale: Sepsis can mimic this presentation but failed hyperoxia and metabolic acidosis with hepatomegaly suggest a duct-dependent lesion that needs immediate prostaglandin therapy. (Source: RCH CPG Cyanotic neonate)

options.text: Give intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg over 30 minutes to correct the lactic acidosis
options.rationale: Bicarbonate does not treat the underlying ductal-dependent circulation; restoring ductal patency with prostaglandin corrects perfusion and resolves acidosis. (Source: RCH CPG Cyanotic neonate)

options.text: Commence dobutamine 5 micrograms/kg/min as inotropic support before any other measure
options.rationale: Inotropes do not restore ductal-dependent pulmonary blood flow and may worsen the shunt; prostaglandin E1 is the priority intervention with cardiology input. (Source: RCH CPG Cyanotic neonate)

explanation.summary: A neonate with cyanosis and failed hyperoxia in the first 48 hours of life has duct-dependent congenital heart disease until proven otherwise. Prostaglandin E1 reopens or maintains the ductus arteriosus and restores pulmonary or systemic blood flow until echocardiography defines anatomy and surgical or catheter intervention is arranged. Side effects to anticipate during prostaglandin therapy include apnoea, hypotension and fever. Septic workup and broad spectrum antibiotics may be added because sepsis can mimic duct-dependent collapse.
key_points: Failed hyperoxia means SpO2 stays under 85% on 100% oxygen.
Start prostaglandin E1 at 10 nanograms/kg/min and titrate.
Anticipate apnoea, hypotension and fever as side effects.
Echocardiography defines the lesion and guides intervention.
explanation.pearls: Prostaglandin E1 causes apnoea in about 10 to 15 percent of neonates; have a clinician at the bedside able to intubate before starting infusion, particularly during interhospital retrieval.

subtopic_detail: Bacterial tracheitis recognition in croup that fails to respond to adrenaline
stem: A 4-year-old boy presents to ED with worsening 'croup'. He had 3 days of viral upper respiratory illness with mild barking cough, then today rapidly deteriorated with high fever, severe stridor at rest, copious thick yellow secretions and a hoarse voice. He has had no benefit from two doses of nebulised adrenaline (5 mL of 1:1000) given 30 minutes apart and 0.6 mg/kg oral dexamethasone given on arrival. He is now sitting forward, drooling, looking toxic and frightened. His parents have not refused any care.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 156, RR 44, SpO2 90% on 2 L/min nasal prongs, BP 96/56, T 39.5, weight 18 kg
data_table.Examination: Toxic-looking, sitting forward, biphasic stridor, suprasternal and intercostal recession, hoarse voice, no rash, no obvious foreign body, copious purulent secretions
data_table.Investigations: Not yet - prioritising airway
data_table.Medications: Two doses nebulised adrenaline, oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg, paracetamol given

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Repeat nebulised adrenaline 5 mL 1:1000 and a second dose of dexamethasone, then reassess in 30 minutes for response
options.rationale: Failure of two doses of nebulised adrenaline plus dexamethasone in a toxic child with purulent secretions and high fever points away from viral croup; further croup therapy delays definitive airway management. (Source: RCH CPG: Stridor)

options.text: Obtain a lateral neck X-ray to look for a thumb-print sign of epiglottitis or steeple sign of croup before any airway intervention
options.rationale: Imaging in a child with imminent airway compromise delays definitive airway control and has no place ahead of expert intubation; airway team activation is the priority. (Source: RCH CPG: Stridor)

options.text: Perform an awake nasendoscopy at the bedside to inspect the supraglottis for swollen cherry-red epiglottis and exclude epiglottitis
options.rationale: Awake examination of the supraglottis in a distressed child with critical upper airway disease risks complete obstruction; airway inspection occurs after controlled anaesthesia in theatre. (Source: RCH CPG: Stridor)

options.text: Call anaesthesia, ENT and intensive care for theatre intubation, then send tracheal cultures and start intravenous flucloxacillin plus ceftriaxone
options.rationale: Bacterial tracheitis (a viral croup that does not respond to adrenaline, with toxicity, high fever, and purulent secretions) requires senior airway team-led controlled intubation in theatre, then tracheal toilet, microbiological sampling and empirical antibiotics covering Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus pneumoniae. (Source: RCH CPG: Stridor)

options.text: Administer high-flow nasal oxygen at 2 litres per kilogram per minute and observe for 1 hour to allow steroid effect before considering intubation
options.rationale: HFNC does not relieve fixed upper airway obstruction from purulent secretions; waiting for steroid effect in a toxic deteriorating child risks loss of the airway. (Source: RCH CPG: Stridor)

explanation.summary: Bacterial tracheitis classically follows a viral URTI with worsening croup that does not respond to nebulised adrenaline or steroids. The child is toxic, febrile, and has purulent tracheal secretions, distinguishing from viral croup (less toxic, dry barking cough) and epiglottitis (sudden onset, drooling, no cough). Management is airway team-led controlled intubation in theatre with simultaneous tracheal toilet, cultures, and intravenous flucloxacillin (Staph aureus is the dominant pathogen in Australia) plus ceftriaxone for Streptococcus pneumoniae and Haemophilus influenzae cover.
key_points: Suspect when croup unresponsive to adrenaline + steroids.
Toxic child, high fever, purulent secretions.
Definitive airway in theatre with anaesthesia + ENT + ICU.
Empirical flucloxacillin + ceftriaxone IV.
explanation.pearls: Tracheal cultures grow Staphylococcus aureus in 50 percent of Australian cases; once intubated, mucus plugs need repeat bronchoscopic toilet and extubation usually proceeds at 3 to 7 days after resolution of secretions and fever.

stem: You are the ED RMO seeing an 8-month-old infant brought in by her mother for worsening breathing. She has had 3 days of coryza and cough. Today the mother reports feeding has dropped to about 50% of usual volume and the last wet nappy was three hours ago. The infant is alert but distressed.

data_table.Vital signs: RR 62, SpO2 92% room air, HR 158, T 37.8 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Subcostal and intercostal recession, fine bilateral crackles, no wheeze, mucous membranes tacky
data_table.Investigations: Not yet performed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Admit for inpatient observation with supplemental oxygen as needed and paced nasogastric feeds
options.rationale: Sustained SpO2 below 92% on room air, RR over 60, and feeding under 50% of normal meet RCH admission criteria for moderate bronchiolitis; supportive oxygen and paced feeds are first-line. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Trial inhaled salbutamol via spacer and formally reassess work of breathing after twenty minutes
options.rationale: Bronchodilators do not improve outcomes in typical viral bronchiolitis in infants under 12 months and are not recommended. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Administer a single oral prednisolone dose and observe in the emergency department for one hour
options.rationale: Systemic corticosteroids confer no benefit in acute bronchiolitis and are not used routinely. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Discharge home with written safety netting and a planned GP review the following morning
options.rationale: Discharge is unsafe with reduced feeding, reduced wet nappies, and borderline oxygenation, which together indicate moderate severity requiring admission. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Administer nebulised adrenaline 5 mg with a planned repeat dose at thirty minutes if needed
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline is not routinely recommended and does not change admission rates or length of stay. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Australian paediatric practice classifies bronchiolitis severity by oxygenation, respiratory effort, feeding, and hydration. Moderate severity meets admission criteria. Supportive care with oxygen and paced feeds is first-line; bronchodilators, steroids, and nebulised adrenaline are not recommended for typical viral bronchiolitis under 12 months. The discriminator here is recognising that feeding under 50% combined with borderline SpO2 mandates admission.
key_points: RCH admission triggers include SpO2 under 92%, RR over 60, feeds under half, or apnoeas
Bronchodilators, steroids, and adrenaline are not routinely indicated under 12 months
Paced NG feeds preserve hydration without increasing aspiration risk
explanation.pearls: RCH bronchiolitis pathway uses SpO2 below 92% as the supplemental oxygen threshold; do not start oxygen at higher saturations as it prolongs admission.

stem: You are the neonatology fellow planning autumn discharge for an ex-29-week preterm girl, now 6 months corrected age and 4.8 kg, with established bronchopulmonary dysplasia requiring 0.25 L/min home oxygen at discharge. Her family lives in regional Victoria. RSV season is about to start in 4 weeks. Her older brother attends day care and the family includes a 2-year-old cousin sharing the household. Her mother asks what can be done to reduce her risk of severe bronchiolitis this season.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7°C, HR 130, RR 48 on 0.25 L/min via nasal prongs, SpO2 95%
data_table.Examination: Weight 4.8 kg (3rd centile), mild intercostal recession, scattered fine inspiratory crackles, no acute respiratory distress
data_table.Investigations: Maternal antenatal RSV vaccination was not given (vaccine eligibility had not been reached before delivery), most recent chest X-ray: chronic interstitial changes

lead_in: What is the most appropriate prevention strategy?

options.text: Commence oral montelukast 4 mg nightly to reduce post-bronchiolitis wheeze across the season
options.rationale: Montelukast is not an RSV prophylactic and does not prevent severe RSV bronchiolitis admission. (Source: GINA Australia and Therapeutic Guidelines)

options.text: Administer monthly intramuscular palivizumab for the duration of the local RSV season
options.rationale: Palivizumab is being replaced by single-dose nirsevimab in Australian jurisdictions because nirsevimab provides season-long protection in one dose. (Source: AIH)

options.text: Single weight-based intramuscular nirsevimab dose before the start of the RSV season
options.rationale: AU NIRP (immunisation handbook) recommends single-dose nirsevimab for high-risk infants under 2 years with chronic lung disease of prematurity or significant CHD entering their first or second RSV season, replacing monthly palivizumab in most jurisdictions. (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook NIRP)

options.text: Standard hygiene and exposure-avoidance advice without any specific RSV pharmacoprophylaxis
options.rationale: Behavioural measures alone are insufficient in an infant with BPD on home oxygen; targeted monoclonal-antibody prophylaxis substantially reduces hospital admission. (Source: AIH)

options.text: Vaccinate the infant with the maternal recombinant RSV vaccine RSVpreF intramuscular dose
options.rationale: RSVpreF is a maternal vaccine given antenatally; it is not licensed for direct infant vaccination. (Source: AIH)

explanation.summary: Nirsevimab is a long-acting monoclonal antibody recommended in Australia for infants entering their first RSV season and for high-risk infants (chronic lung disease of prematurity, haemodynamically significant congenital heart disease) entering their second RSV season. A single weight-based IM dose covers the entire season, replacing monthly palivizumab where funded. Maternal vaccination with RSVpreF is the alternative pathway for healthy term babies whose mothers were eligible antenatally.
key_points: Single-dose nirsevimab covers the whole RSV season.
Eligible: first season for all infants, second season for high-risk infants.
Maternal RSVpreF in pregnancy is an alternative for healthy term infants.
explanation.pearls: If maternal RSVpreF was given in pregnancy at 28-36 weeks, the term infant does not also need nirsevimab; if not, a single nirsevimab dose is the AU default.

stem:
A male infant born at 39+0 weeks by elective caesarean section without labour is admitted to the special care nursery at 1 hour of life for ongoing respiratory distress. Birthweight 3520 g, APGARs 8 and 9. He developed tachypnoea, mild grunting and intercostal recession from 15 minutes of age. He is now 4 hours old on nasal high-flow at 6 L/min with FiO2 0.30, maintaining SpO2 96 percent preductal and 96 percent postductal.

Mother had a negative group B Strep screen, no fever, no prolonged rupture of membranesplications; no meconium-stained liquor. T 36.9 deg C, HR 152, RR 78, BP 62/40 with capillary refill 2 seconds and warm peripheries. Chest is clear with no asymmetry. Chest radiograph at 30 minutes showed hyperinflation, prominent perihilar streaking, fluid in the right horizontal fissure, and no air bronchograms or focal consolidation.

He has not yet fed. Blood gas: pH 7.32, pCO2 48 mmHg, BE -2. Sepsis bloods are pending. You are confident this is transient tachypnoea of the newborn (TTN).

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 deg C, HR 152 bpm, RR 78 /min, SpO2 97 percent on high-flow FiO2 0.25
data_table.Examination: Mild grunting, intercostal recession, equal air entry bilaterally, no asymmetry, no scaphoid abdomen, normal peripheral pulses
data_table.Imaging: CXR: hyperinflation, prominent perihilar vascular streaking, fluid in the right horizontal fissure, no air bronchograms, no focal consolidation, no pneumothorax

lead_in: Which next management step is most appropriate?

options.text: Continue nasal high-flow titrated to SpO2 91-95 percent, nil by mouth on IV maintenance, reassess at 72 hours
options.rationale: TTN is managed supportively with non-invasive respiratory support (nasal high-flow or CPAP) titrated to keep SpO2 91 to 95 percent, gut rest until work of breathing settles, and IV maintenance fluids while waiting for foetal lung fluid to clear (usually within 24 to 72 hours). Persisting tachypnoea beyond 72 hours mandates reconsideration of the diagnosis (sepsis, cardiac, surfactant deficiency), per RCH CPG: Neonatal respiratory distress and Safer Care Victoria neonatal eHandbook.

options.text: Administer endotracheal surfactant via the INSURE technique now, given the persisting FiO2 requirement at 4 hours of postnatal life
options.rationale: Surfactant therapy is for respiratory distress syndrome (preterm, ground-glass with air bronchograms, surfactant deficiency); the CXR pattern and term gestation here are inconsistent with RDS, and surfactant is not indicated for TTN, per RCH CPG: Neonatal respiratory distress.

options.text: Commence empirical IV benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin pending blood culture results and continue the current respiratory support level
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics in a term infant with negative maternal GBS, no fever, no prolonged ROM and a CXR pattern characteristic of TTN are not routinely required; antibiotics are reserved for clinical concern for sepsis or persisting illness beyond 72 hours, per RCH CPG: Early onset sepsis.

options.text: Wean nasal high-flow off now and commence demand breastfeeding to encourage foetal lung-fluid clearance and feeding tolerance
options.rationale: Weaning support and commencing feeds while the infant remains in significant respiratory distress (RR 78, grunting, FiO2 0.30) risks aspiration and worsens work of breathing; nil by mouth and continued support are appropriate until respiratory effort settles, per RCH CPG: Neonatal respiratory distress.

options.text: Urgently transfer to a tertiary neonatal intensive care unit for elective intubation and mechanical ventilation now
options.rationale: The infant is stable on non-invasive support with adequate saturations and a normal blood gas; intubation and tertiary transfer are reserved for failing non-invasive support, persistent hypoxaemia, or alternative diagnoses, per RCH CPG: Neonatal respiratory distress.

explanation.summary: TTN results from delayed clearance of foetal lung fluid, classically after elective caesarean without labour. It resolves within 24 to 72 hours with supportive oxygen and CPAP if needed. Key CXR differentiators: RDS = ground-glass with air bronchograms (preterm); MAS = patchy coarse infiltrates with hyperinflation (term with meconium); pneumonia = focal consolidation; pneumothorax = hyperlucent hemithorax.
key_points: TTN: term, caesarean without labour, perihilar streaks and fissural fluid
RDS: preterm, diffuse ground-glass, surfactant deficiency
MAS: term post-dates, meconium liquor, patchy infiltrates
Pneumothorax: asymmetric breath sounds, hyperlucent on transillumination and CXR
explanation.pearls: If tachypnoea persists beyond 72 hours, reconsider the diagnosis.

stem:
You are the paediatric registrar in a regional emergency department reviewing a 5-month-old previously well term girl on day 3 of coryza, cough and fast breathing. She is taking around 60% of her usual breastfeed volumes, has not vomited, and her last wet nappy was 3 hours ago. Initial observations showed mild subcostal recession with SpO2 92% in air; after suction of clear nasal secretions and a 90 mL breastfeed she is alert, smiling, with no recession at rest and SpO2 of 95% in air for 30 minutes.

The mother is calm, has a working car, lives 25 minutes from the hospital, has a clear plan to seek review, and there is good GP follow-up the next morning.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 138, RR 44, SpO2 95% in air (after suction and feed), T 37.6 degrees C, capillary refill under 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Mild wheeze with prolonged expiration, no chest wall recession at rest, good air entry, no focal crackles, mucous membranes moist
data_table.Investigations: Bedside nasopharyngeal aspirate viral PCR pending; no other investigations performed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate disposition?

options.text: Admit her for a chest x-ray and intravenous benzylpenicillin until viral nasopharyngeal aspirate PCR results return, given the fever and the possibility of an early bacterial lower respiratory tract co-infection
options.rationale: Routine chest imaging and empirical antibiotics are not indicated in uncomplicated bronchiolitis without focal signs or persistent high fever; both increase harm without altering outcomes in this clinical picture. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Discharge home with a written bronchiolitis action plan, simple analgesia for fever, advice on feed volumes and red-flag symptoms, and review by her general practitioner within 24 hours
options.rationale: She meets discharge criteria: SpO2 at or above 92% in air sustained for at least 30 minutes, mild work of breathing, feeding above 50% of baseline, no apnoeas, reliable carer and access to review; an action plan with red-flag advice and short-interval GP review is standard practice. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Admit her to the paediatric ward for nasogastric feeding at two-thirds maintenance and continuous pulse oximetry monitoring for at least 24 hours regardless of her current observations and ongoing feeding
options.rationale: Routine nasogastric feeding is reserved for infants taking under 50% of usual oral intake or in respiratory distress that precludes safe oral feeding, neither of which applies here. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Discharge her home only after a 4-hour high-flow nasal cannula trial at 2 L/kg/min in the ED short stay unit to confirm she does not develop any escalating oxygen requirement before going home
options.rationale: High-flow nasal cannula is an escalation therapy for infants with persistent hypoxia or severe work of breathing despite low-flow oxygen, not a pre-discharge screening test for a stable, normoxic infant. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Admit her for a trial of nebulised hypertonic 3% saline every 6 hours and a single inhaled salbutamol challenge to assess for any bronchodilator-responsive component of her wheeze before discharge
options.rationale: Nebulised hypertonic saline and trial salbutamol are not recommended in routine bronchiolitis management in infants under 12 months and do not change the disposition decision in a stable child meeting discharge criteria. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Disposition in bronchiolitis is driven by sustained SpO2, work of breathing, feeding adequacy, apnoeas and carer factors rather than by interventions of unproven benefit. This infant meets all RCH discharge criteria after simple suction and a feed. Hypertonic saline, bronchodilators, antibiotics and routine imaging are not indicated. Safe discharge requires a clear action plan and short-interval review.
key_points: Discharge thresholds: SpO2 at or above 92% in air sustained 30 minutes, feeds at or above 50% baseline, no apnoea, mild work of breathing
Hypertonic saline and salbutamol are not recommended in routine bronchiolitis under 12 months
Antibiotics and chest x-ray only with focal signs, persistent high fever or severe disease
Carer reliability, distance to care, and follow-up plan are part of the disposition decision
explanation.pearls: If SpO2 sits at 92% in air after suction and feed in an otherwise well infant with reliable carers, discharge with a written action plan and GP review within 24 hours is correct - admitting to escalate to high-flow is over-treatment.

stem: You are the paediatric resident reviewing a 10-week-old non-Aboriginal infant brought in by ambulance with three days of coryza and worsening cough. He is breastfeeding only every five hours instead of every three. He has had no apnoeas reported by the parents. His older sibling has the same illness. Birth was at term and there is no congenital cardiac history.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6 °C, HR 158, RR 62, SpO2 92% room air
data_table.Examination: Mild subcostal recession, audible wheeze, widespread fine crackles, capillary refill 2 seconds, normal fontanelle, no rash
data_table.Investigations: Nasopharyngeal aspirate respiratory syncytial virus positive; chest x-ray not performed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge home with parental education on feeding cues and warning signs and arrange general practice review the following morning
options.rationale: Reduced feeding to under half normal and oxygen saturation of 92% on room air meet admission thresholds for bronchiolitis in Australia; discharge home is unsafe. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Admit for nasogastric or intravenous hydration with supplemental oxygen titrated to maintain saturations above 92% and ongoing observation
options.rationale: Admission criteria are met by tachypnoea over 60, hypoxia under 92% on room air and feeding under half normal; supportive care with hydration and oxygen is the mainstay of bronchiolitis management. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Trial inhaled salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer and reassess after 20 minutes before deciding on further management of his work of breathing
options.rationale: Salbutamol does not improve outcomes in bronchiolitis under 12 months because bronchospasm is not the predominant pathology; routine bronchodilator use is discouraged. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Administer oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg now and arrange a chest x-ray to assess for bacterial complication before deciding on admission
options.rationale: Corticosteroids have no role in viral bronchiolitis and routine chest x-ray adds radiation without changing management in classical disease. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Start empiric oral amoxicillin 30 mg/kg three times daily for five days to cover bacterial superinfection and arrange paediatric clinic review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Routine antibiotics are not indicated in classical bronchiolitis with a known viral cause and no focal consolidation or persistent fever. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Bronchiolitis is the most common reason for hospital admission in Australian infants under one year. Management is supportive: oxygen for hypoxia, nasogastric or intravenous fluids for reduced feeding, and observation for apnoea. Bronchodilators, corticosteroids, antibiotics and chest x-rays are not part of routine care. Admission thresholds include oxygen saturation under 92%, tachypnoea over 60, feeding under half normal, parental concern, apnoea, and high social risk.
key_points: Admission criteria include SpO2 under 92%, feeding under half normal, apnoea or moderate respiratory distress
Salbutamol, corticosteroids and antibiotics are not routinely indicated
Nasogastric fluids are preferred over intravenous when tolerated
High-flow nasal oxygen is reserved for those failing standard oxygen
Apnoea risk is greatest in infants under 6 weeks of age
explanation.pearls: High-flow nasal cannula oxygen at 2 L/kg/min reduces escalation to intensive care in moderately severe bronchiolitis but is reserved for failure of standard oxygen, not first-line.

subtopic_detail: Sickle cell vaso-occlusive crisis - initial management
stem:
A 7-year-old boy with known HbSS sickle cell disease presents to a metropolitan ED with 6 hours of severe pain in both thighs and the lower back. He has had three similar admissions in the past 18 months. He is afebrile, with no cough, no chest paindominal pain. He takes daily penicillin V prophylaxis, folic acid and hydroxyurea, and his vaccinations including conjugate pneumococcal, meningococcal B and ACWY, Hib and annual influenza are up to date.

He last had a transfusion 9 months ago. On examination he is alert and engaged, tearful with pain, with no scleral icterus, a soft non-tender abdomen, no chest signs and no neurological deficit. There is no swelling of joints, no warmth and no focal tenderness over long bones beyond diffuse muscular tenderness. Initial observations: T 36.9 degC, HR 118 bpm, RR 22, SpO2 98% on room air, BP 108/64 mmHg.

Bedside SpO2 trend over the last 30 minutes is stable. He has not taken any analgesia at home and is rating his pain 9 out of 10.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 degC, HR 118, RR 22, SpO2 98% RA, BP 108/64.
data_table.Examination: Distressed by pain, no chest signs, no focal bony tenderness, no scleral icterus, no neurology.
data_table.Investigations: Not yet performed.
data_table.Medications: Penicillin V prophylaxis, folic acid, hydroxyurea.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Oral ibuprofen alone, then reassess pain at 60 minutes
options.rationale: Oral NSAID monotherapy is inadequate for severe (9/10) sickle pain; strong opioid within 30 min alongside multimodal is required. Would be correct only for mild pain (Source: RCH CPG: Sickle Cell Disease).

options.text: IV saline bolus 20 mL/kg, observe without giving analgesia
options.rationale: Bolus fluids are not first-line in uncomplicated VOC and risk pulmonary oedema/ACS; maintenance fluids do not replace timely opioid. Would be correct only for confirmed hypovolaemia (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: BSH guideline: Acute management of sickle cell pain

options.text: IV morphine plus paracetamol and ibuprofen, maintenance fluids
options.rationale: Severe vaso-occlusive crisis requires a strong opioid (IV or intranasal) within 30 minutes, combined with paracetamol and an NSAID if no contraindication, plus maintenance (not bolus) fluids. Oxygen is given only if SpO2 falls below 95% (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).

options.text: Empirical IV ceftriaxone, blood cultures and ward admission
options.rationale: He is afebrile with no infective focus; empirical antibiotics are for sickle patients at 38 degC or higher. Would be correct with fever 38 or higher (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Urgent exchange transfusion via the on-call haematologist
options.rationale: Exchange transfusion is for ACS, stroke, multi-organ failure or refractory priapism, not uncomplicated VOC. Would be correct in those indications (Source: BSH; RCH CPG).
source_refs: BSH guideline: Acute management of sickle cell pain

explanation.summary: Uncomplicated paediatric vaso-occlusive crisis is managed with timely strong opioid analgesia (target within 30 minutes), multimodal paracetamol and NSAID, maintenance IV fluids, and supplemental oxygen only if SpO2 falls below 95% (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).
key_points: Strong opioid within 30 minutes for severe sickle pain
Maintenance fluids, not boluses, in uncomplicated VOC
Oxygen only if SpO2 < 95% to avoid masking acute chest syndrome
Reassess every 30 minutes for the first 2 hours
Screen for triggers: dehydration, infection, hypoxia, cold
explanation.pearls: Bolus fluids and high-flow oxygen in a normoxic child with sickle pain can both precipitate acute chest syndrome - give what is needed, not more.

sources.label: BSH guideline: Acute management of sickle cell pain

subtopic_detail: SCID red flags - recognition and urgent investigation
stem:
A 4-month-old boy is brought to a regional ED for his third admission, this time with 10 days of persistent oral thrush despite two courses of oral nystatin, an erythematous nappy-area candidiasis, and a 5-week history of chronic non-bloody diarrhoea with poor weight gain (current weight on the 3rd centile, having tracked along the 50th at birth). His past admissions in the last 6 weeks have been for pneumocystis-like interstitial pneumonia requiring nasal high-flow and one episode of CMV viraemia.

He was born at term to non-consanguineous parents; his mother had a brother who died of an unexplained pneumonia at 7 months of age. He received BCG at birth in his country of birth, and rotavirus oral vaccine at 6 weeks in Australia. On examination he is small and pale, with oral plaques, perineal candidiasis, no thymic shadow on a recent chest X-ray, no palpable lymph nodes or tonsillar tissue, and bilateral fine inspiratory crackles.

T 37.4 degC, HR 152, RR 50, SpO2 94% RA. FBC today: Hb 96, WCC 5.2 with absolute lymphocyte count 0.6 x10^9/L (well below the age-specific lower limit of around 3.0). Total IgG, IgA, IgM are all low.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.4 degC, HR 152, RR 50, SpO2 94% RA.
data_table.Examination: Failure to thrive, oral thrush, perineal candidiasis, no thymic shadow on CXR, absent tonsils/lymph nodes, bilateral crackles.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 96, WCC 5.2, ALC 0.6 x10^9/L, IgG / IgA / IgM all low.
data_table.Medications: Recent rotavirus oral vaccine, BCG at birth overseas.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate single next step?

options.text: Empirical oral fluconazole and clinic review in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Persistent candidiasis is the surface feature of likely SCID; fluconazole alone misses the underlying immunodeficiency and time-critical transplant pathway. Would never be correct (Source: ASCIA Primary Immunodeficiency).

options.text: Same-day immunology referral, lymphocyte subsets, isolation, no live vaccines
options.rationale: The presentation is highly suspicious for severe combined immunodeficiency: failure to thrive, severe lymphopenia (ALC < 2.5-3.0), opportunistic infection (Pneumocystis-like pneumonia, CMV viraemia, persistent candidiasis), maternal family history of unexplained infant death, BCG exposure, and absent thymic shadow. Urgent immunology referral, T/B/NK lymphocyte subsets, reverse isolation, withholding live vaccines and use of irradiated CMV-negative leucodepleted blood products are time-critical (ASCIA: SCID; eviQ).

options.text: Reassure parents that recurrent infections are normal at 4 months
options.rationale: Pneumocystis-like pneumonia, CMV viraemia, FTT, persistent candidiasis and severe lymphopenia together are not normal at any age; urgent specialist evaluation required. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Recurrent Infections).

options.text: Give routine 6-month MMR catch-up vaccination at this visit
options.rationale: Live vaccines (MMR, rotavirus, BCG, varicella) are contraindicated in suspected SCID and cause disseminated infection. Would never be correct (Source: NCIRS).

options.text: Send HIV antibody (not PCR) and clinic review in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Maternal-acquired HIV antibody persists under 18 months regardless of status; HIV PCR is the test of choice. Would be correct only in older children (Source: ASHM).

explanation.summary: SCID is a paediatric emergency. Red flags include severe lymphopenia (ALC under age-specific lower limit, often below 2.5 x10^9/L), failure to thrive, opportunistic infection (PCP, CMV, persistent candidiasis), absent thymic shadow, and family history of early unexplained infant death. Urgent immunology referral, reverse isolation, withholding live vaccines, and irradiated CMV-negative leucodepleted blood products are time-critical; haematopoietic stem cell transplant in the first 3.5 months of life carries a markedly better prognosis (ASCIA: SCID).
key_points: Severe lymphopenia at any age: think SCID and check subsets
Withhold live vaccines: rotavirus, BCG, MMR, varicella
Use irradiated CMV-negative leucodepleted blood products
Early HSCT before 3.5 months improves survival markedly
Family history of unexplained infant infectious death is a red flag
explanation.pearls: If a 4-month-old has had Pneumocystis pneumonia, the diagnosis is SCID until immunology proves otherwise.

subtopic_detail: Bronchiolitis severity and respiratory support escalation
stem: You are the paediatric resident on the inpatient ward reviewing a 7-week-old term infant on day 4 of a coryzal illness. He is breastfeeding only 40% of usual volumes and has had two episodes of brief apnoea overnight without colour change. He has no risk factors for chronic lung disease and is fully immunised for age. The night nursing team have escalated because of increasing work of breathing despite low-flow nasal prong oxygen at 1 L/min.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6 °C, HR 168, RR 64, SpO2 92% on 1 L nasal prongs, BP 78/48 mmHg, capillary refill 3 seconds
data_table.Examination: Moderate subcostal and intercostal recession, tracheal tug, widespread fine inspiratory crackles, mild expiratory wheeze, alert but tiring, anterior fontanelle soft
data_table.Investigations: Nasopharyngeal PCR positive for RSV, capillary blood gas pH 7.31, pCO2 56 mmHg, HCO3 24 mmol/L, lactate 1.4 mmol/L, no chest radiograph performed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in respiratory support?

options.text: Continue low-flow nasal prong oxygen at 1 L/min, encourage smaller more frequent breastfeeds and repeat the capillary blood gas in two hours before deciding on escalation
options.rationale: Persistent hypercapnia with sustained tachypnoea over 60 and recurrent apnoea identifies severe bronchiolitis; further passive observation risks decompensation before the next gas. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence inhaled salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer every 20 minutes and add nebulised hypertonic saline 3% three times daily to reduce mucus plugging and airway resistance
options.rationale: Australian guidance recommends against routine bronchodilators, nebulised hypertonic saline, corticosteroids or antibiotics in viral bronchiolitis under 12 months. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Switch to humidified high-flow nasal cannula at 8 L/min with FiO2 titrated to keep SpO2 above 92% and place the infant on continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring in a paediatric ward bed
options.rationale: Standard high-flow weight-based flow for infants is 2 L/kg/min capped near the body weight in early bronchiolitis. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Arrange immediate intubation and mechanical ventilation in the paediatric intensive care unit given the apnoeic episodes and rising carbon dioxide on the capillary blood gas reading
options.rationale: A graded escalation through high-flow with PICU liaison precedes intubation; intubation is reserved for failure of non-invasive support or recurrent apnoea with desaturation. (Source: PREDICT bronchiolitis evidence statement 2024)

options.text: Commence humidified high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min with FiO2 titrated to maintain SpO2 above 92% in a monitored high-dependency bed and liaise early with paediatric retrieval and intensive care
options.rationale: Weight-based high-flow (2 L/kg/min, around 9 L/min for a 4.5 kg infant) is first-line escalation in moderate-severe bronchiolitis; recurrent apnoea and a pCO2 over 50 mandate HDU-level monitoring with early retrieval liaison. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Bronchiolitis severity is judged by feeding, work of breathing, oxygen requirement and apnoea rather than chest signs alone. Severe disease is defined by SpO2 under 92% in air, sustained tachypnoea over 60, marked recession, recurrent apnoea or rising pCO2. High-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min is the standard Australian first-line escalation beyond low-flow oxygen. Bronchodilators, steroids, nebulised hypertonic saline and antibiotics do not change outcomes in uncomplicated viral bronchiolitis. Early liaison with paediatric retrieval is required when infants need HDU-level support.
key_points: Severe bronchiolitis: SpO2 under 92%, RR over 60, marked recession, apnoea, pCO2 over 50
HFNC starts at 2 L/kg/min; flow is not titrated to oxygen saturation
Apnoea under 8 weeks of age is a retrieval and HDU indication
Routine salbutamol, steroids and antibiotics are not recommended in bronchiolitis
Chest radiograph is not required unless atypical features or complications are suspected
explanation.pearls: In infants under 8 weeks with bronchiolitis the apnoea risk peaks around day 3-5; co-bedded continuous SpO2 plus respiratory rate trending detects deterioration earlier than intermittent observations.

subtopic_detail: Tetralogy of Fallot hypercyanotic spell first-line manoeuvre
stem: An 8-month-old boy known to have unrepaired tetralogy of Fallot is brought to the emergency department after a 10-minute episode of inconsolable crying, then deep cyanosis. He is now distressed, deeply blue and his mother says his murmur has become quieter. Cardiac surgery is planned in 2 weeks. He has no fever and no recent infections.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168, RR 56, SpO2 62 percent on room air, T 36.9
data_table.Examination: Marked central cyanosis, soft systolic murmur (previously harsh), no respiratory distress signs out of proportion
data_table.Investigations: Capillary blood gas pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate first manoeuvre?

options.text: Administer intravenous adrenaline 10 mcg per kg to support cardiac output during the spell
options.rationale: Adrenaline increases right ventricular outflow tract spasm by beta-agonism and worsens the spell; beta-blockade is the relevant pharmacology. (Source: RCH CPG Cyanotic Heart Disease)

options.text: Begin chest compressions and bag-mask ventilation as the first response to the deep cyanosis present
options.rationale: The child is not in cardiac arrest; aggressive resuscitation without addressing the shunt physiology will not resolve the spell. (Source: RCH CPG Cyanotic Heart Disease)

options.text: Commence inhaled nitric oxide via face mask to dilate the pulmonary vasculature immediately now
options.rationale: Pulmonary vasodilation is not the mechanism in tet spells; the problem is right ventricular outflow tract obstruction with shunt reversal. (Source: RCH CPG Cyanotic Heart Disease)

options.text: Provide intravenous frusemide 1 mg per kg to reduce preload and lung congestion in this child
options.rationale: Tet spells are not driven by pulmonary oedema; diuresis reduces preload and worsens the shunt. (Source: RCH CPG Cyanotic Heart Disease)

options.text: Place the child in the knee-to-chest position and give high-flow oxygen via face mask
options.rationale: Knee-to-chest increases systemic vascular resistance, reverses right-to-left shunting and is the immediate first step in a tet spell, with simultaneous high-flow oxygen. (Source: RCH CPG Cyanotic Heart Disease)

explanation.summary: A tet spell results from acute increase in right ventricular outflow tract obstruction combined with falling systemic vascular resistance, causing right-to-left shunting and profound hypoxia. Immediate management is to settle the child in knee-to-chest (or parental cuddling with knees flexed) plus high-flow oxygen. Pharmacological escalation includes morphine 0.1 mg/kg IM or IV to abolish hyperpnoea, IV phenylephrine to raise SVR, IV propranolol to relax the RVOT, sodium bicarbonate for acidosis and prompt referral for surgical or palliative shunting if recurrent. Quiet murmur indicates near-total RVOT obstruction.
key_points: Knee-to-chest + high-flow O2 are immediate first steps.
Morphine 0.1 mg/kg blunts hyperpnoea.
Phenylephrine raises SVR and reduces shunt.
Propranolol relaxes RVOT muscular obstruction.
Quieter murmur means worse spell.
explanation.pearls: Beta-blockade with propranolol is used both acutely (intravenously) and as outpatient prophylaxis (oral 1 to 2 mg/kg/day) in babies awaiting repair - schedule surgical repair before 6 months when spells start to recur to avoid long-term right ventricular damage.

stem: You are the paediatric registrar in a tertiary ED reviewing a 6-year-old girl brought in by ambulance with an acute asthma exacerbation. She has a known background of mild-persistent asthma on a low-dose budesonide preventer and salbutamol as needed. Paramedics gave continuous nebulised salbutamol and ipratropium en route. On arrival 20 minutes ago she received oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg and a further three back-to-back salbutamol nebulisers. She is now sitting forward, talking in short phrases, with persistent moderate work of breathing.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 158 bpm, RR 38, SpO2 92% on 6 L/min via nebuliser, BP 102/64, T 37.1°C
data_table.Examination: Tracheal tug, intercostal recession, bilateral wheeze with prolonged expiratory phase, air entry reduced at both bases, no focal crackles
data_table.Investigations: Venous blood gas: pH 7.32, pCO2 48 mmHg, HCO3 22 mmol/L, lactate 3.8 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Intravenous hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg as a slow bolus and continue current nebulised therapy
options.rationale: Oral prednisolone has equivalent bioavailability and onset to IV hydrocortisone; she has already received an adequate corticosteroid dose, so switching steroid route is not the right escalation. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

options.text: Intramuscular adrenaline 10 micrograms/kg into the lateral thigh and observation for response
options.rationale: Adrenaline IM is reserved for anaphylaxis or near-arrest asthma where IV access is unavailable; this child has wheeze without urticaria, hypotension or angio-oedema. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

options.text: Intravenous aminophylline 10 mg/kg loading dose over 60 minutes on the general paediatric ward
options.rationale: Aminophylline is a third-line option used only after IV magnesium has failed and is given in PICU because of arrhythmia risk; jumping to it now skips a safer escalation. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

options.text: Switch to high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min FiO2 0.5 and reassess in 30 minutes
options.rationale: HFNC does not deliver bronchodilator therapy and risks delaying definitive treatment in a child with rising pCO2; nebulised salbutamol via a tight-fitting mask remains the preferred delivery route during escalation. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg infused over 20 minutes with cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: Severe asthma not responding to inhaled bronchodilators and oral corticosteroid escalates to IV magnesium sulfate as the next step before intubation; the rising pCO2 with persistent hypoxia and lactate signals failing ventilation despite maximal nebulised therapy. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

explanation.summary: Children with severe acute asthma who do not respond to back-to-back nebulised salbutamol, ipratropium and adequate systemic corticosteroid require escalation. The standard Australian paediatric escalation is intravenous magnesium sulfate, followed by IV salbutamol or aminophylline infusions in a high-dependency or PICU setting if magnesium fails. A rising pCO2 in a tiring child despite high-dose bronchodilator therapy is an ominous sign of impending respiratory failure and mandates HDU or PICU transfer.
key_points: IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg over 20 minutes is the standard next step after failed inhaled therapy plus systemic steroid in paediatric severe asthma.
Oral prednisolone 1 to 2 mg/kg has equivalent onset to IV hydrocortisone in children who tolerate oral therapy.
Rising pCO2 in an asthmatic child is a pre-terminal sign and demands HDU or PICU level care.
explanation.pearls: In paediatric severe asthma a venous pCO2 above 45 mmHg with persistent tachypnoea is the signal to give IV magnesium sulfate within 30 minutes and call the PICU.

stem: You are the GP registrar reviewing a 10-month-old boy at a follow-up visit two weeks after his first hospital admission for viral wheeze. His mother says the cough has resolved but he still has occasional noisy breathing when he gets a cold. There is no family history of atopy and he has no eczema. His father, who has well-controlled adult asthma, is asking whether the baby should start a preventer to stop further admissions.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 120 bpm, RR 28, SpO2 99% on room air, T 36.8°C
data_table.Examination: Alert, well-perfused, normal work of breathing, clear chest, no eczema, growth on the 50th centile
data_table.Investigations: Discharge summary: confirmed RSV positive, responded to oxygen and supportive care, no salbutamol response documented

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management advice?

options.text: Start a daily low-dose inhaled budesonide preventer via spacer for the next twelve weeks
options.rationale: Inhaled corticosteroids in infants under 12 months with viral wheeze and no atopy do not reduce admission rates and expose the infant to potential growth effects without clear benefit. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma)

options.text: Refer urgently to a paediatric respiratory physician for cystic fibrosis sweat testing
options.rationale: A single bronchiolitic admission with normal growth and no chronic cough does not raise concern for cystic fibrosis; newborn screening already reduces residual risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma)

options.text: Prescribe a montelukast 4 mg granule sachet nightly during the winter respiratory virus season
options.rationale: Montelukast has only modest benefit in selected preschool wheezers older than 2 years and is not first-line in infants; behavioural and neuropsychiatric adverse effects further limit routine use. (Source: AMH; eTG: Respiratory)

options.text: Trial regular salbutamol 100 micrograms twice daily via spacer through the winter period
options.rationale: Regular salbutamol in infants without documented bronchodilator response is unhelpful and may mask deterioration; in this child salbutamol gave no benefit during admission. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma)

options.text: Reassure the family, continue supportive care and review only if a further severe episode occurs
options.rationale: Under 12 months of age viral wheeze rarely represents atopic asthma and a documented poor salbutamol response confirms the bronchiolitis phenotype; routine preventers are not indicated and watchful waiting with safety-net advice is the standard approach. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma)

explanation.summary: Most wheeze in the first year of life is viral and not steroid-responsive, even when a parent has atopic asthma. Australian guidance reserves a preventer trial for children over 12 months with frequent episodes, interval symptoms or atopic features. Salbutamol responsiveness should be documented before chronic bronchodilator therapy is prescribed to infants. Parental anxiety is best managed with a written action plan and clear escalation triggers rather than empirical preventers.
key_points: Infant viral wheeze without atopy or salbutamol response does not justify a preventer trial.
Preventer trials become appropriate after 12 months when atopic features or interval symptoms appear.
Document bronchodilator response before prescribing regular salbutamol in any infant.
explanation.pearls: Inhaled corticosteroids reduce neither admissions nor symptom days in non-atopic infants under 12 months with isolated viral wheeze.

stem:
A 4-month-old male infant born at term is brought to the regional emergency department in mid-July by his mother. He has had three days of clear coryza and a dry cough, and over the past 24 hours he has been working harder to breathe and is taking only about half of his usual bottle volume. He has had no apnoeas, his nappies are still wet but less so, and he is fully immunised to the current Australian schedule.

He has no significant past medical history and no known sick contacts apart from an older sibling with a runny nose. He is alert and consolable in his mother's arms but visibly tachypnoeic, with audible coarse breath sounds.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6 deg C, HR 165 bpm, RR 60 /min, SpO2 90% on room air, BP 88/54 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Subcostal and intercostal recessions, widespread fine bilateral inspiratory crackles, mild expiratory wheeze, capillary refill 2 seconds, anterior fontanelle soft
data_table.History: Sibling with URTI 5 days ago, no recent travel, no household smoking, term birth at 39 weeks, breast and formula mixed-fed

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg via oxygen-driven nebuliser, reassess work of breathing at 20 minutes
options.rationale: Bronchodilators are not recommended in classic bronchiolitis under 12 months and have not shown benefit in trials or in the RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis.

options.text: Admit for supportive care with oxygen titrated to SpO2 of at least 92% and nasogastric feeds at two-thirds maintenance
options.rationale: This infant meets RCH admission criteria (SpO2 < 92%, feeding under 50% of normal, moderate work of breathing). Management is supportive with oxygen and NG or IV hydration as per RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis.

options.text: Single dose of oral dexamethasone 3.6 mg and arrange GP follow-up with safety-net advice in 24 hours
options.rationale: Corticosteroids do not alter the course of bronchiolitis and are not recommended in any major paediatric guideline, as per RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis.

options.text: Commence IV ceftriaxone 300 mg empirically for presumed secondary bacterial lower respiratory tract infection in this infant
options.rationale: Antibiotics are not indicated in typical bronchiolitis; bacterial co-infection is uncommon in this age group as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory.

options.text: Discharge home on room air with written safety-net advice and arrange GP review within the next 24 hours
options.rationale: Discharge is inappropriate while SpO2 is below 92% and feeding is markedly reduced; admission is required per RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis.

explanation.summary: Acute bronchiolitis is a viral LRTI of infants under 12 months, most commonly RSV in the Australian winter. Management is supportive: oxygen for SpO2 < 92%, hydration via NG or IV fluids, minimal handling. Bronchodilators, corticosteroids, antibiotics, hypertonic saline, and routine CXR are not recommended.
key_points: RCH admission criteria include SpO2 < 92%, apnoea, feeding < 50% of normal, moderate or severe work of breathing, or social concerns
RSV is the commonest cause; PCR is not routinely required
High-flow nasal cannula oxygen is second-line if standard oxygen fails
Antibiotics, bronchodilators, and steroids have no benefit
explanation.pearls: Apnoea may be the only sign in infants under 6 weeks; have a low threshold to admit.

stem:
A 7-year-old girl with known persistent asthma is brought to a tertiary ED by ambulance after 24 hours of escalating wheeze and cough. Her usual preventer is fluticasone 100 microg BD via spacer, with salbutamol as reliever; she has had two previous ED presentations this year and one PICU admission at age 5 requiring high-flow oxygen. At home over the last 6 hours her mother has given hourly salbutamol 12 puffs via spacer with no benefit.

In the ambulance she received back-to-back nebulised salbutamol 5 mg with ipratropium 250 microg every 20 minutes for three doses, plus IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg. There has been minimal symptomatic or objective improvement on arrival. She has had no fevers, no foreign-body history, and no recent oral steroid course in the past 6 weeks.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 162 bpm, RR 44 /min, SpO2 89% on 8 L/min via nebuliser mask, BP 102/68 mmHg, T 37.2 deg C
data_table.Examination: Sitting forward, single-word speech, marked accessory muscle use, silent chest in upper zones, GCS 15 but visibly exhausted
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.28, pCO2 48 mmHg, HCO3 22 mmol/L, lactate 3.1 mmol/L, K 3.2 mmol/L, glucose 8.4 mmol/L
data_table.Medications: Fluticasone 100 microg BD, salbutamol PRN, no LABA, no recent oral prednisolone
data_table.History: Previous PICU admission age 5, two ED visits this year, allergic rhinitis, no NSAID exposure

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Subcutaneous adrenaline 220 microg into the lateral thigh, reassess and repeat in 5 minutes if no response
options.rationale: SC adrenaline is reserved for anaphylaxis-related bronchospasm or settings where inhaled therapy cannot be delivered; it is not first-line escalation in known severe asthma per RCH CPG: Asthma acute.

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 1.1 g (max 2 g) over 20 minutes with continuous cardiac and BP monitoring
options.rationale: After back-to-back salbutamol/ipratropium and systemic steroid have failed in severe acute asthma, IV magnesium sulfate is the next escalation as per RCH CPG: Asthma acute and Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory.

options.text: Rapid sequence intubation and mechanical ventilation with permissive hypercapnia in the emergency department now
options.rationale: Intubation in severe asthma is high risk for bronchospasm, hypotension and barotrauma; it is reserved for impending respiratory arrest after maximal medical therapy, per RCH CPG: Asthma acute.

options.text: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mg via oxygen-driven nebuliser and observe for stridor reduction over 30 minutes
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline targets upper airway oedema in croup, not lower airway bronchospasm; it has no role in this presentation per RCH CPG: Croup.

options.text: Switch from IV hydrocortisone to oral prednisolone 22 mg and continue back-to-back nebulised salbutamol
options.rationale: She has already received an adequate dose of IV hydrocortisone; switching to oral steroid adds no acute benefit and delays escalation, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory.

explanation.summary: Severe / life-threatening acute paediatric asthma escalates as oxygen + back-to-back salbutamol + ipratropium + systemic corticosteroid, then IV magnesium sulfate, then IV salbutamol or aminophylline infusion, with early PICU referral as per RCH CPG: Asthma acute.
key_points: Severity is assessed by speech, accessory muscle use, mental state, SpO2 and air entry
Silent chest is a sign of life-threatening asthma
IV magnesium dose is 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) over 20 minutes
Avoid intubation if at all possible; involve PICU/anaesthetics early
explanation.pearls: Previous PICU admission is a strong predictor of fatal asthma; escalate early and involve PICU before the chest goes truly silent.

stem: A 3-month-old boy with bronchiolitis admitted 18 hours ago to a metropolitan paediatric ward has deteriorated. He has been on high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L per kg per minute with FiO2 0.4 for 6 hours. Despite this his work of breathing has increased with marked subcostal and intercostal recession; he is now only intermittently rousable. His latest capillary blood gas shows pH 7.20, pCO2 9.1 kPa, HCO3 22 mmol/L, lactate 2.4 mmol/L. He is being suctioned hourly and has stopped feeding orally. The treating registrar phones the local paediatric intensive care service.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 188, RR 72, SpO2 92% on HFNC FiO2 0.4, Temp 37.6
data_table.Examination: Marked subcostal and intercostal recession; only intermittently rousable; sluggish capillary refill 3s
