data_table.Investigations: Capillary blood gas pH 7.20 pCO2 9.1 kPa HCO3 22 lactate 2.4; chest X-ray shows hyperinflation with peri-hilar streaking

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Increase HFNC flow to 3 L per kg per minute and continue current FiO2 with reassessment in 60 minutes before any other escalation
options.rationale: HFNC has clearly failed with rising CO2 and altered mental state; further uptitration delays definitive ventilatory support. (Source: ANZICS Paediatric Respiratory Support Guidance)

options.text: Trial nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg back-to-back for 30 minutes before any further respiratory support escalation step
options.rationale: Bronchodilators have no place in bronchiolitis and do not address the central failure of ventilation in this scenario. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Prepare for intubation and arrange paediatric intensive care retrieval team transfer to a tertiary paediatric ICU
options.rationale: Bronchiolitis with rising CO2, reduced mental state and HFNC failure has an indication for intubation and PICU transfer; AU paediatric retrieval services should be engaged early. (Source: ANZICS Paediatric Respiratory Support Guidance)

options.text: Commence non-invasive bilevel ventilation locally and continue oral feeds while awaiting tertiary paediatric ICU advice
options.rationale: BiPAP can be a bridge in some centres, but with reducing GCS and ongoing rise in CO2 a controlled intubation is preferred before deterioration. (Source: ANZICS Paediatric Respiratory Support Guidance)

options.text: Administer intravenous methylprednisolone 1 mg per kg and start broad-spectrum antibiotics empirically for bacterial superinfection
options.rationale: Steroids do not modify bronchiolitis and empirical antibiotics are not indicated without a clear focus; the immediate problem is ventilatory failure. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Severe bronchiolitis in infants is the commonest reason for paediatric ICU admission with viral illness. AU practice tiers respiratory support from oxygen via nasal prongs, to HFNC, to non-invasive ventilation (in selected sites), to intubation; HFNC failure markers include rising CO2, reducing GCS, increasing work of breathing, and tachycardia. Once HFNC failure is recognised, the treating team should escalate to PICU rather than uptitrate flow indefinitely. The paediatric retrieval service should be contacted early to facilitate inter-hospital transfer.
key_points: HFNC failure markers: rising CO2, reducing GCS, persistent tachypnoea and tachycardia
Escalation pathway is to NIV or intubation, not higher HFNC
Engage paediatric retrieval services early for inter-hospital transfer
Bronchodilators, steroids, and antibiotics have no role even in severe bronchiolitis
explanation.pearls: Rising pCO2 above 8 kPa with reducing GCS on HFNC in bronchiolitis is an indication for intubation and PICU transfer, not further HFNC uptitration.

subtopic_detail: Moderate croup with stridor at rest and mild recession
stem: You are the paediatric registrar in a regional emergency department reviewing a 22-month-old boy brought in by his mother at 0200 with a 1-day history of coryza followed by a sudden barking cough and noisy breathing on waking from sleep. He is upset and crying intermittently. On examination he has audible inspiratory stridor at rest, mild suprasternal and intercostal recession but no tracheal tug, and is well perfused with a respiratory rate of 36 and oxygen saturations of 97% in air. He has not had previous croup episodes and has no congenital airway concerns.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.4 degrees C, HR 138, RR 36, SpO2 97% in air, CRT 1 second
data_table.Examination: Inspiratory stridor at rest, mild recession, voice hoarse but cry strong, chest clear
data_table.Investigations: Nil performed (clinical diagnosis)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Give 0.15 mg/kg oral dexamethasone now and observe the child for at least four hours after the dose to assess clinical response before considering discharge home with a written croup advice sheet for the family
options.rationale: RCH and Therapeutic Guidelines recommend single-dose oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg as first-line for moderate croup with at-least 4 hours of observation; nebulised adrenaline is reserved for severe disease with marked recession or tracheal tug. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis))

options.text: Administer nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 via mask immediately and arrange for paediatric high-dependency admission with continuous monitoring for at least 24 hours regardless of the response to the initial nebulised dose
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline is reserved for severe croup with marked sternal recession, tracheal tug or hypoxia; mild recession with stridor at rest is moderate and treated with steroid alone in most cases. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis))

options.text: Take a nasopharyngeal aspirate for respiratory viral PCR, perform a lateral soft-tissue neck X-ray to look for the steeple sign and withhold any pharmacological treatment until imaging and laboratory results have returned
options.rationale: Croup is a clinical diagnosis and routine imaging or viral PCR delays treatment without changing management; steroids should be given promptly on clinical grounds alone. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis))

options.text: Administer high-flow humidified oxygen via nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min and a single intramuscular dose of 50 mg/kg ceftriaxone empirically pending the result of a paired blood culture and full blood count
options.rationale: Croup is viral so antibiotics are not indicated; humidified oxygen is unhelpful in an infant with saturations of 97% and may distress the child further. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis))

options.text: Discharge home immediately with written advice on steam-bath therapy and indications to return, without any pharmacological intervention because the child is comfortably saturating well and the stridor will likely settle overnight
options.rationale: Even mild croup benefits from a single dose of oral dexamethasone to reduce relapse and re-presentation; discharge without steroid is suboptimal in stridor-at-rest disease. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis))

explanation.summary: Moderate croup is defined as stridor at rest with mild to moderate recession. First-line treatment is single-dose oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg (or prednisolone 1 mg/kg if dexamethasone unavailable). Nebulised adrenaline (0.5 mL/kg of 1:1000, max 5 mL) is added for severe disease with marked recession, tracheal tug, agitation or hypoxia. Children are observed for at least 4 hours post-steroid (or 4 hours post-last-adrenaline) before discharge. Antibiotics, imaging and routine humidification are not recommended.
key_points: Croup severity: mild (barking cough, no stridor at rest), moderate (stridor at rest, mild recession), severe (marked recession, tracheal tug, agitation)
Oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg for all moderate or severe croup
Add nebulised adrenaline for severe croup
Observe 4 hours post-treatment before discharge
Croup is a clinical diagnosis - no routine imaging
explanation.pearls: A child with croup who becomes drooling, very still and toxic should raise suspicion of bacterial tracheitis or epiglottitis - call ENT/anaesthetics for theatre intubation before any throat examination.

subtopic_detail: Paediatric anaphylaxis dose, route and observation
stem: You are the paediatric registrar in a busy emergency department called to triage to assess a 6-year-old girl who developed lip swelling, generalised urticaria and a hoarse voice within 10 minutes of eating a cashew from a snack bag at her brother's birthday party 25 minutes ago. She has a history of mild asthma controlled with a salbutamol inhaler used twice in the last year, no known allergies and no chronic medication. On arrival she is anxious, has audible inspiratory stridor, wheeze on auscultation, widespread urticaria and is centrally well perfused.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 142, RR 36, SpO2 94% in air, BP 92/56 mmHg, T 36.8 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Audible stridor, bilateral wheeze, widespread urticaria, mild peri-orbital oedema, alert
data_table.Investigations: Bedside observations as above; weight estimated at 22 kg

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate treatment?

options.text: Administer 10 mg of intramuscular promethazine and 200 mg of intravenous hydrocortisone as first-line therapy and observe for two hours of clinical improvement before re-evaluating the need for further pharmacological treatment
options.rationale: Antihistamines and corticosteroids are adjuncts only and never replace adrenaline for any feature of anaphylaxis; delaying IM adrenaline increases mortality. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines)

options.text: Give intravenous adrenaline 1 mcg/kg as a slow push followed by a continuous infusion at 0.05 mcg/kg/min, intubate immediately for the stridor and admit her directly to the paediatric intensive care unit for ongoing airway management
options.rationale: IV adrenaline is reserved for refractory anaphylaxis after two IM doses with persistent shock and senior clinician supervision; first-line is always IM adrenaline. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines)

options.text: Administer 0.3 mg of 1:1000 adrenaline by intramuscular injection into the mid anterolateral thigh now, lay her supine with legs elevated, give high-flow oxygen and observe for at least 4 hours after symptoms resolve before considering discharge
options.rationale: ASCIA action plan: IM adrenaline 0.01 mg/kg of 1:1000 (max 0.5 mg) into the mid anterolateral thigh, with supine positioning, high flow oxygen and a minimum 4-hour observation after symptom resolution before discharge with an adrenaline autoinjector and follow up. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines)

options.text: Give 10 puffs of inhaled salbutamol via spacer and 5 mL nebulised adrenaline 1:1000, then reassess in 15 minutes and only proceed to intramuscular adrenaline if wheeze and stridor have not resolved with these inhaled therapies
options.rationale: Inhaled salbutamol treats wheeze but not airway oedema or hypotension; nebulised adrenaline is for severe upper airway oedema as an adjunct only, never replacing IM adrenaline. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines)

options.text: Administer an immediate 20 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride intravenously and observe blood pressure response before deciding whether intramuscular adrenaline is required, given that the systolic blood pressure remains above 90 mmHg
options.rationale: Anaphylaxis is diagnosed by any combination of skin, airway or breathing involvement after exposure regardless of blood pressure; IM adrenaline is given immediately rather than awaiting hypotension. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Anaphylaxis is diagnosed when there is any acute onset of typical skin or mucosal change PLUS either respiratory or cardiovascular involvement, or any hypotension after a known allergen. First-line treatment is IM adrenaline 0.01 mg/kg of 1:1000 (10 mcg/kg, max 0.5 mg) into the mid anterolateral thigh, repeated every 5 minutes as needed. Supine positioning, high flow oxygen and IV fluid 20 mL/kg if hypotensive complete the bundle. Observation for at least 4 hours post symptom resolution is required, with adrenaline autoinjector script, action plan and allergy referral on discharge.
key_points: IM adrenaline first - always first - 0.01 mg/kg 1:1000 IM mid anterolateral thigh, max 0.5 mg
Repeat IM adrenaline every 5 minutes if no response
Supine with legs elevated unless pregnant (left lateral) or vomiting (lateral)
Antihistamines and steroids are adjuncts only
Observe at least 4 hours post symptom resolution
explanation.pearls: For a 22 kg child the IM dose is 0.22 mg, but ASCIA pre-calibrates by age band: 0.15 mg for under 20 kg, 0.30 mg for 20 to 50 kg and 0.5 mg for over 50 kg - the band is appropriate here despite slight under-dose.

stem:
A 4-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with a 24-hour history of progressively worsening shortness of breath and a tight, non-productive cough. He has a known history of atopy, including eczema and a peanut allergy, and has had two previous hospital admissions for viral-induced wheeze. His parents report he had a coryzal illness starting three days ago. He received six puffs of salbutamol via a spacer every three hours at home with minimal effect.

On examination, he is alert but agitated, sitting forward and speaking in single words. He has a prominent tracheal tug, severe intercostal recessions, and widespread biphasic wheeze with reduced air entry bilaterally. He is immediately commenced on high-flow oxygen, continuous nebulised salbutamol, nebulised ipratropium bromide, and intravenous hydrocortisone. One hour later, he remains severely distressed with poor air entry.

data_table.Vital signs: Initial: HR 165 bpm, RR 48/min, SpO2 90% (room air). Current (1hr): HR 175 bpm, RR 45/min, SpO2 92% (8 L/min O2 mask)
data_table.Examination: Agitated, single words, severe work of breathing, poor air entry.
data_table.Medications: Continuous nebulised salbutamol, ipratropium, IV hydrocortisone given in ED.

lead_in: What is the single best next intervention?

options.text: Administer a stat dose of intramuscular adrenaline.
options.rationale: No anaphylaxis features (urticaria, angioedema, hypotension) despite peanut history; adrenaline is not indicated here. Would be correct only with confirmed anaphylaxis features (Source: ASCIA Acute Anaphylaxis).

options.text: Commence an intravenous continuous aminophylline infusion.
options.rationale: Aminophylline is third-line in paediatric severe asthma, only after IV magnesium and IV salbutamol have failed. Would be correct as third-line in PICU (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma Acute).

options.text: Intubate and commence mechanical ventilation immediately.
options.rationale: Intubation in severe asthma is fraught with complications (bronchospasm, hypotension, barotrauma) and reserved for absolute respiratory arrest or exhaustion. Would be correct only with impending arrest after maximal medical therapy (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Administer a single bolus of intravenous magnesium sulfate.
options.rationale: In a child with severe acute asthma failing continuous nebulised therapy and systemic corticosteroids, a bolus of intravenous magnesium sulfate is the next recommended step. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma Acute)

options.text: Administer a fluid bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride.
options.rationale: Asthmatic children may be mildly dehydrated, but fluid boluses do not relieve bronchospasm. Would be correct only for confirmed hypovolaemia, not primary asthma treatment (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: This child has severe acute asthma refractory to first-line therapies (continuous bronchodilators and corticosteroids). The next appropriate step in the Australian step-wise management of paediatric status asthmaticus is an intravenous bolus of magnesium sulfate. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma Acute)
key_points: Severe asthma is characterised by agitation, inability to talk in sentences, severe work of breathing, and SpO2 < 92%.
Initial escalation involves bursts of inhaled bronchodilators transitioning to continuous nebulisers if no improvement.
When continuous nebulisers fail, the first-line intravenous adjunct is magnesium sulfate, followed by an IV salbutamol infusion if needed in an ICU setting.
explanation.pearls: A 'silent chest' or poor air entry indicates critically severe bronchospasm where wheeze may not be audible due to minimal airflow.

stem:
An 8-month-old ex-35-week infant is brought to the emergency department in winter with 4 days of cough, coryza, and poor feeding. He was reviewed by a GP yesterday and diagnosed with bronchiolitis. Overnight he took less than half his usual feeds and had two episodes of post-tussive vomiting. His mother reports increasing work of breathing and fewer wet nappies today.

He has no history of congenital heart disease but required CPAP briefly after birth. In the emergency department he is started on low-flow oxygen via nasal prongs with transient improvement in saturations. Thirty minutes later he remains tachypnoeic with persistent subcostal and intercostal recession. Capillary blood gas shows mild hypercapnia without acidosis.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168 bpm, RR 62/min, BP 92/58 mmHg, Temp 37.8 C, SpO2 89% room air improving to 93% on 2 L/min oxygen
data_table.Examination: Moderate subcostal and intercostal recession, diffuse crackles with scattered wheeze, dry mucous membranes, capillary refill 2 seconds
data_table.Investigations: CBG pH 7.34, pCO2 48 mmHg, HCO3 24 mmol/L
data_table.Medications: No regular medications, immunisations up to date

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence high-flow nasal prong therapy and continue supportive management with nasogastric hydration
options.rationale: Persistent hypoxia and moderate work of breathing despite low-flow oxygen indicate need for escalation to high-flow nasal prong therapy. Supportive care remains the cornerstone of bronchiolitis management. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Trial inhaled salbutamol and reassess respiratory rate before considering respiratory support escalation
options.rationale: Routine bronchodilator trials are not recommended in typical bronchiolitis (no reliable improvement). Would be correct only in older viral wheeze (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Commence intravenous ceftriaxone because the hypercapnia suggests developing bacterial pneumonia
options.rationale: Mild hypercapnia may occur with worsening bronchiolitis and does not suggest bacterial pneumonia; no focal findings or septic features. Would be correct with focal pneumonia features (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Arrange urgent intubation because preterm birth increases risk of sudden respiratory decompensation
options.rationale: Prematurity increases severity risk but this infant does not show impending respiratory arrest, severe acidosis or exhaustion. Would be correct only with impending failure (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Discharge home with frequent oral fluids because oxygen saturations improved on nasal prongs
options.rationale: Persistent tachypnoea, increased WOB, poor intake and oxygen requirement make discharge unsafe. Would be correct only without those features (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: This infant has moderate-to-severe bronchiolitis with persistent respiratory distress despite low-flow oxygen. Australian bronchiolitis guidance recommends escalation to high-flow nasal prong therapy with supportive hydration rather than bronchodilators or antibiotics in typical disease. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)
key_points: Bronchiolitis management is primarily supportive. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)
Poor feeding and persistent work of breathing are indications for admission and escalation of respiratory support. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Respiratory)
explanation.pearls: Scattered wheeze alone does not make bronchodilator-responsive asthma more likely in typical infant bronchiolitis. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

stem:
A 5-month-old boy presents during winter with 3 days of cough, rhinorrhoea, and poor feeding. Today his parents noticed increased work of breathing and reduced wet nappies. He was born at term with no neonatal complications. His older sibling recently had a viral upper respiratory illness. On arrival he is alert but feeding poorly and taking only half his usual volumes.

Examination reveals diffuse crackles and wheeze bilaterally with moderate subcostal recession. A medical student asks whether salbutamol nebulisers should be trialled because wheeze is prominent on auscultation.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 37.8 C, HR 154 bpm, RR 54/min, BP 88/50 mmHg, SpO2 90% room air
data_table.Examination: Moderate work of breathing, diffuse crackles and wheeze, moist mucous membranes
data_table.Investigations: No investigations performed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence supplemental oxygen and supportive care with feeding assessment
options.rationale: Supportive care including oxygen and hydration assessment is the mainstay of bronchiolitis management when hypoxia and feeding difficulty are present. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Administer repeated salbutamol nebulisers because wheeze indicates likely reactive airways disease
options.rationale: Routine bronchodilators are not recommended in typical bronchiolitis (limited benefit). Would be correct only in older viral wheeze (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone because moderate respiratory distress predicts steroid responsiveness
options.rationale: Corticosteroids not routinely recommended for uncomplicated bronchiolitis. Would be correct only in croup or asthma (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Arrange chest radiography because diffuse crackles suggest bacterial pneumonia requiring antibiotics
options.rationale: Routine CXR not indicated in typical bronchiolitis and may increase unnecessary antibiotic use. Would be correct only with focal consolidation suspicion (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Discharge home after nasal suction because oxygen saturations commonly fluctuate during viral illnesses
options.rationale: Persistent hypoxia and poor feeding warrant inpatient supportive management rather than discharge. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: This infant has typical bronchiolitis with hypoxia and feeding difficulty. The reasoning challenge is avoiding unnecessary bronchodilator or steroid use and recognising that supportive care remains the evidence-based cornerstone of management. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)
key_points: Oxygen and hydration assessment are key components of bronchiolitis management. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)
Routine bronchodilators and corticosteroids are not recommended in uncomplicated bronchiolitis. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)
explanation.pearls: Prominent wheeze in bronchiolitis often tempts clinicians toward asthma-style therapy despite limited evidence of benefit. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

stem:
A 7-year-old boy with known moderate persistent asthma presents to the emergency department with an acute exacerbation. He has had a coryzal illness for 3 days, and his asthma has been worsening despite increasing his fluticasone propionate preventer to 250 mcg BD and using salbutamol 6 puffs via MDI and spacer every 2-3 hours. On arrival, he is unable to complete sentences in one breath, speaking only 2-3 words at a time.

He is sitting upright with marked suprasternal and intercostal recession, and audible expiratory wheeze. His RR is 42, HR is 135 bpm, and SpO2 is 90% on room air. His PEFR is unmeasurable due to poor technique.

data_table.Vital signs: RR 42, HR 135 bpm, SpO2 90% RA, Temp 37.2°C, BP 105/65 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Unable to complete sentences (2-3 words), sitting upright, marked suprasternal and intercostal recession, audible expiratory wheeze, PEFR unmeasurable
data_table.Investigations: Nil performed at this stage
data_table.Medications: Fluticasone propionate 125 mcg BD (usual); Salbutamol 6 puffs via MDI + spacer given by mother prior to arrival

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management in the emergency department?

options.text: Salbutamol 12 puffs (100 mcg/puff) via MDI/spacer with ipratropium 4 puffs (21 mcg/puff) via MDI/spacer, plus oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg immediately
options.rationale: MDI-spacer is acceptable delivery but severe/life-threatening asthma (SpO2 90, unable to speak, marked recession) requires OXYGEN-DRIVEN nebuliser. Would be correct only in mild-moderate asthma (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Asthma).

options.text: Hudson mask oxygen 8 L/min to SpO2 >94%, nebulised salbutamol 5 mg plus ipratropium 250 mcg by O2-driven nebuliser, oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg, repeat 20 min
options.rationale: This child has severe acute asthma (SpO2 90%, unable to speak in sentences, marked recession). Management includes: oxygen to maintain SpO2 ≥94%, oxygen-driven nebulised salbutamol (5 mg for >5 years) plus ipratropium (250 mcg) for the first hour (3 doses at 20-minute intervals), and oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg (max 50 mg). Continuous clinical assessment is essential. If there is inadequate response after the first hour, escalate to IV magnesium sulfate, IV salbutamol, or aminophylline, and involve ICU. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook; RCH CPG: Acute Asthma)

options.text: High-flow nasal prong oxygen at 2 L/kg/min, IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg, urgent chest X-ray to exclude pneumonia before any bronchodilator therapy commences now
options.rationale: IV hydrocortisone is alternative not superior to oral pred; bronchodilator must NOT be delayed for CXR which is not routine. Would be correct only if oral not tolerated (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg over 20 min as first-line, oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg, nasal prong O2 at 2 L/min to maintain SpO2 >92%
options.rationale: IV magnesium is SECOND-line after failed initial inhaled therapy. Would be correct as second-line after failure (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg alone via O2-driven nebuliser, oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg, observe one hour, then add ipratropium and consider IV therapy
options.rationale: Salbutamol 2.5 mg is for 2-5 years; a 7-year-old requires 5 mg; ipratropium must be ADDED from start in severe disease. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: This 7-year-old has acute severe (bordering on life-threatening) asthma: SpO2 90%, unable to speak in sentences, marked recession, and audible wheeze. The immediate management priorities are: oxygen to target SpO2 ≥94%, oxygen-driven nebulised salbutamol 5 mg plus ipratropium 250 mcg repeated every 20 minutes for the first hour (3 doses), and oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg. Continuous assessment of response is essential. If inadequate response after the first hour, escalation to IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg, IV salbutamol infusion, or aminophylline infusion, and ICU consultation is warranted. Chest X-ray is not routinely indicated.
key_points: Classification of acute asthma severity: mild (SpO2 >92%, sentences), moderate (SpO2 >92%, phrases), severe (SpO2 <92%, words, recession), life-threatening (SpO2 <92% with silent chest, cyanosis, exhaustion). (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)

First-line for severe asthma: oxygen, nebulised salbutamol (5 mg if >5 yrs, 2.5 mg if 2-5 yrs) PLUS ipratropium 250 mcg, repeated every 20 min for first hour, and oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Asthma)
Ipratropium added to salbutamol in the first hour significantly reduces hospitalisation in children with moderate-severe asthma. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)
IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) is second-line for severe asthma unresponsive to initial inhaled therapy. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Asthma)
explanation.pearls: In severe paediatric asthma, a silent chest is more dangerous than a wheezy chest. A silent chest with SpO2 <90% is life-threatening asthma - call a code blue, prepare for intubation, and give IV salbutamol and magnesium.

stem: A 6-year-old boy with known asthma presents with acute severe exacerbation. He has received 3 doses of nebulised salbutamol (5 mg) and ipratropium (250 mcg) over 1 hour, plus oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg. His SpO₂ remains 89% on high-flow oxygen, he speaks in single words, and has severe recessions. Chest is tight, and air entry is reduced. What is the most appropriate next step?

data_table.Vital signs: SpO₂ 89% on O₂ 15 L/min, HR 150 bpm, RR 40
data_table.Examination: Single words, severe recessions, reduced air entry bilaterally
data_table.Investigations: None
data_table.Medications: Nebulised salbutamol/ipratropium x3, oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Administer IV magnesium sulfate (max 2 g) over 20 minutes
options.rationale: For severe acute asthma unresponsive to initial inhaled bronchodilators and steroids, IV magnesium sulfate is the recommended second-line therapy. It acts as a smooth muscle relaxant and may prevent intubation. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook; RCH CPG: Acute Asthma)

options.text: Commence IV salbutamol infusion and transfer to ICU
options.rationale: IV salbutamol is third-line after magnesium failure or in life-threatening asthma; magnesium tried first. Would be correct as third-line (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook).

options.text: Repeat the nebulised salbutamol and ipratropium every 20 minutes for another hour
options.rationale: After 3 doses without improvement, further inhaled therapy alone is unlikely sufficient; escalation needed. Would be correct only with early response (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook).

options.text: Give an IV fluid bolus of 20 mL/kg and reassess
options.rationale: IV fluids may be given for hydration but will not relieve bronchospasm; magnesium is the specific bronchodilator. Would be correct as adjunct, not next step (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook).

options.text: Obtain a chest X-ray to exclude pneumothorax
options.rationale: Pneumothorax is differential but immediate priority is escalation of bronchodilator therapy; CXR can follow stabilisation. Would be correct only with high pneumothorax suspicion (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook).

explanation.summary: This child has severe life-threatening asthma not responding to maximal inhaled therapy and steroids. The next step is IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg, which has evidence for reducing hospitalisation and preventing intubation. IV salbutamol is reserved for refractory cases. Chest X-ray and fluids are secondary.
key_points: IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) is second-line for severe acute asthma. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)
IV salbutamol or aminophylline are third-line. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Asthma)
Indications for escalation include persistent severe respiratory distress, SpO₂ <90% despite oxygen, and failure to improve after initial bronchodilator therapy. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)
explanation.pearls: In severe asthma, if the patient is tiring, a silent chest is an ominous sign – call ICU immediately. Magnesium is not a substitute for intubation when it is needed.

stem: A 12-year-old boy with known peanut allergy develops anaphylaxis within minutes of ingesting a cookie containing peanut. He received intramuscular adrenaline 0.5 mg twice (5 minutes apart), intravenous normal saline boluses (total 60 mL/kg), and an intravenous adrenaline infusion at 0.1 mcg/kg/min, which has been titrated to 0.5 mcg/kg/min. Despite this, he remains hypotensive at 70/40 mmHg, tachycardic at 150 bpm, and has cold, mottled extremities with an SpO₂ of 85% on high-flow oxygen.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 70/40, HR 150, SpO₂ 85% ventilated, mottled
data_table.Examination: Ventilated, cold extremities, no urticaria now, poor perfusion
data_table.Investigations: Normal glucose, electrolytes; pH 7.15, lactate 6 mmol/L
data_table.Medications: Adrenaline infusion 0.5 mcg/kg/min, IV fluids, IM adrenaline x2

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological intervention?

options.text: Administer intravenous methylene blue over 20 minutes, as it may improve refractory vasoplegic shock in anaphylaxis
options.rationale: Methylene blue has case reports in refractory anaphylactic shock but is not first-line rescue in AU; vasopressin preferred. Would be correct as later salvage (Source: ASCIA).

options.text: Give intravenous glucagon 1 mg bolus, as the patient is likely on beta-blocker therapy
options.rationale: Glucagon is useful in beta-blocker patients with anaphylaxis; no beta-blocker history here. Would be correct with beta-blocker history (Source: ASCIA).

options.text: Administer intravenous hydrocortisone 200 mg for its rapid vasopressor effect
options.rationale: Corticosteroids have delayed onset (hours) and do not provide immediate haemodynamic support. Would be correct only as adjunct, not for acute haemodynamics (Source: ASCIA).

options.text: Start intravenous vasopressin infusion at 0.01 units/min
options.rationale: Adding a second vasopressor such as vasopressin or noradrenaline is recommended in refractory anaphylactic shock when adrenaline infusion and fluid resuscitation have not restored perfusion; vasopressin counters the vasoplegia from histamine release. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines; ANZCOR Anaphylaxis)

options.text: Increase the adrenaline infusion to a higher rate
options.rationale: Higher adrenaline doses may cause arrhythmias and are less effective in anaphylactic vasoplegia; vasopressin provides different mechanism. Would be correct only at moderate doses (Source: ASCIA).

explanation.summary: Refractory anaphylactic shock not responding to maximised adrenaline infusion and aggressive fluid resuscitation warrants addition of a second vasopressor (vasopressin or noradrenaline) while continuing adrenaline. Glucagon is for patients on beta-blockers. Methylene blue is a published rescue option but is not first-line Australian therapy. ECMO should be considered if pharmacological measures fail. (Source: ASCIA; ANZCOR)
key_points: Methylene blue 2 mg/kg IV for refractory anaphylactic shock. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis)
Adrenaline remains first-line; methylene blue is rescue therapy. (Source: UpToDate)
explanation.pearls: In refractory anaphylaxis, consider methylene blue if the shock is vasoplegic and not responding to high-dose adrenaline.

subtopic_detail: Bronchiolitis - admission criteria and supportive management
stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 4-month-old, ex-37-week infant in ED with 3 days of coryza progressing to noisy breathing and reduced feeding. The parents report wet nappies have halved and the last feed was 80 mL of formula 5 hours ago, taken with several pauses for breath. The infant is alert when handled but tires. The mother smoked in pregnancy and there are no siblings.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6, HR 162, RR 62, SpO2 93% in room air, BP 88/52, weight 6.3 kg
data_table.Examination: Mild tracheal tug and subcostal recession, fine bilateral crackles with scattered wheeze, capillary refill 2 seconds, anterior fontanelle flat
data_table.Investigations: Nasopharyngeal aspirate positive for RSV, capillary blood gas pH 7.35 pCO2 45 HCO3 22, blood glucose 4.2

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Trial of nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg with reassessment in 20 minutes to determine whether bronchodilator therapy should continue regularly
options.rationale: Salbutamol is not recommended in bronchiolitis under 12 months; trials show no meaningful benefit and may cause tachycardia and desaturation. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 3 days alongside supportive care, given the wheeze component and family history of maternal smoking exposure
options.rationale: Corticosteroids have no role in viral bronchiolitis even when wheeze is present; they do not shorten illness or reduce admission. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Admit for observation with low-flow nasal cannula oxygen, nasogastric or small-volume oral feeds, and minimal handling with routine reassessment of work of breathing
options.rationale: SpO2 below 92%, feeding under 50% baseline and increased work of breathing meet admission criteria; supportive care with oxygen and assisted hydration is standard. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence intravenous amoxicillin and benzylpenicillin empirically while awaiting blood culture results, treating possible bacterial co-infection causing the respiratory distress
options.rationale: Bacterial co-infection is uncommon in confirmed RSV bronchiolitis without focal consolidation or marked fever; antibiotics are not indicated. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Discharge home with parental advice on small frequent feeds, nasal saline drops, and return immediately for any apnoea, cyanosis or worsening work of breathing observed
options.rationale: Hypoxia, reduced feeding and increased work of breathing in a 4-month-old preclude discharge; this infant needs inpatient observation. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Bronchiolitis is a clinical diagnosis in infants under 12 months; investigations are not routinely required. Admission is guided by hypoxia (SpO2 persistently under 92%), feeding under 50% of normal, significant work of breathing, apnoea, or social risk. Therapy is supportive: minimal handling, oxygen if needed and nasogastric or intravenous fluids if feeding is poor. High-flow nasal cannula is escalated when standard oxygen fails. Salbutamol, steroids, hypertonic saline, adrenaline and antibiotics do not change outcomes in routine cases.
key_points: Admit if SpO2 under 92%, feeds under 50% or apnoea episodes.
Supportive care only - no bronchodilators or steroids.
Escalate to high-flow if work of breathing persists on low-flow.
RSV is the most common pathogen, but co-infection is rare.
Chest x-ray is not routine and may prompt unnecessary antibiotics.
explanation.pearls: Apnoea is the dominant deterioration in infants under 6 weeks or ex-premature; admit even with normal saturations if any apnoeic episode is reported by carers.

stem: An 8-month-old infant is brought to the emergency department with 3 days of coryza and an increasing wheeze. He has taken 60% of his usual feeds over the past 12 hours but is still having normal wet nappies. There is no fever and no history of choking. He was born at 38 weeks gestation and has had three previous episodes of viral wheeze. His father has asthma. On arrival he is alert and pink, with mild subcostal recession and a wet productive cough between feeds.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 142, RR 48, SpO2 91% room air, Temp 36.9
data_table.Examination: Mild subcostal recession; bilateral fine inspiratory crackles and expiratory wheeze; CRT 2s; alert
data_table.Investigations: Nasopharyngeal swab pending; chest X-ray not performed

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in his management?

options.text: Admit for supplemental oxygen targeting SpO2 92 to 96% with small-volume oral or nasogastric feeds
options.rationale: AU paediatric guidance is to admit infants with bronchiolitis when SpO2 is below 92% in room air or feeding is below 50% of normal; oxygen is titrated to maintain SpO2 92 to 96% and hydration is supported by nasogastric or small-volume oral feeds. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Administer nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg and reassess clinical response over the next 30 minutes
options.rationale: Bronchodilators are not recommended in infants under 12 months with bronchiolitis as the wheeze reflects small-airway oedema rather than smooth-muscle bronchospasm; trials show no meaningful response. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence inhaled fluticasone via spacer and review with the general practitioner in 48 hours
options.rationale: Inhaled corticosteroids have no role in acute bronchiolitis and do not modify illness duration; a primary-care review at 48 hours would also miss the existing hypoxia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Administer nebulised hypertonic 3% saline three times daily plus a short course of oral prednisolone
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline shows inconsistent benefit and is not part of routine AU paediatric care; oral corticosteroids do not alter outcomes in viral bronchiolitis. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Discharge home with oral amoxicillin to cover likely lower respiratory bacterial co-infection
options.rationale: Routine antibiotics are not indicated as bronchiolitis is viral; bacterial co-infection rates are low and discharge with hypoxia is unsafe. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Bronchiolitis is the commonest cause of hospitalisation in infants under 12 months and is managed almost entirely with supportive care. AU paediatric guidance frames admission decisions around hypoxia, feeding adequacy, and work of breathing rather than imaging or laboratory testing. Bronchodilators, corticosteroids, and antibiotics are not recommended in routine bronchiolitis because the underlying pathology is mucosal oedema and debris rather than bronchospasm or bacterial infection. The shift in oxygen target from 94% to 92% in recent AU guidelines was driven by evidence that the lower threshold reduces length of stay without increasing safety events.
key_points: SpO2 92% in room air is the AU admission threshold for bronchiolitis
Feeding under 50% of usual or significant work of breathing also mandates admission
Bronchodilators, steroids, and antibiotics have no routine role
Nasogastric feeds and minimal handling are preferred over IV fluids when tolerated
explanation.pearls: RCH CPG sets bronchiolitis oxygen target at 92 to 96% and admits feeds below 50% of normal; bronchodilator trials add no value under 12 months.

subtopic_detail: Repeat IM adrenaline at adult dose for school-age child with refractory anaphylaxis
stem:
You are the paediatric emergency registrar reviewing a 6-year-old boy with known peanut allergy who accidentally ate a peanut butter biscuit at a party 10 minutes ago. He has developed urticaria, lip swelling, vomiting once, a hoarse voice, audible expiratory wheeze, and a feeling of throat tightness. His parent administered his prescribed EpiPen Jr (150 mcg adrenaline) into the lateral thigh 5 minutes ago at home with no clear improvement before transfer.

On arrival in the resuscitation cubicle he remains distressed, tachypnoeic and his oxygen saturation has fallen on room air. Weight at his last GP visit was 24 kg.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9, HR 140, RR 36, BP 92/56, SpO2 92% room air
data_table.Examination: Diffuse urticaria, lip and tongue swelling, hoarse voice, audible expiratory wheeze, mild intercostal recession, alert but distressed, weight 24 kg
data_table.Investigations: Bedside glucose 5.6, ECG sinus tachycardia, no IV access yet established, allergy passport documents peanut as known allergen, prior adrenaline auto-injector at home

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Repeat the EpiPen Jr 150 mcg IM into the opposite lateral thigh, reassess in 5 minutes and consider escalation only if no response, while preparing intravenous access and high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask in the resuscitation bay
options.rationale: At 6 years of age and 24 kg, the appropriate adrenaline dose has moved from the 150 mcg auto-injector to 300 mcg IM (1:1000 0.3 mL); repeating the junior dose underdoses. (Source: ASCIA Guidelines: Acute management of anaphylaxis)
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines: Acute management of anaphylaxis

options.text: Administer 0.3 mL of 1:1000 adrenaline (300 mcg) IM into the mid-anterolateral thigh, apply high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask, position the patient supine with legs elevated, and obtain IV access for fluid resuscitation
options.rationale: For a school-age child of 24 kg the standard IM adrenaline dose is 300 mcg (0.3 mL of 1:1000) into mid-anterolateral thigh, repeated every 5 minutes if needed; positioning and oxygen are concurrent. (Source: ASCIA Guidelines: Acute management of anaphylaxis)
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines: Acute management of anaphylaxis

options.text: Administer IV adrenaline 50 mcg bolus immediately under cardiac monitoring as the initial parenteral dose given the failure of the prehospital intramuscular dose to control the patient's airway and respiratory features
options.rationale: IV adrenaline is reserved for refractory anaphylaxis under continuous cardiac monitoring after multiple IM doses have failed; it is not the next step here. (Source: ASCIA Guidelines: Acute management of anaphylaxis)
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines: Acute management of anaphylaxis

options.text: Administer nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg via mask and IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg as primary therapy for the bronchospasm component, with adrenaline reserved as backup if there is no improvement over the next 10 minutes
options.rationale: Adrenaline is the first-line therapy in anaphylaxis; salbutamol and hydrocortisone are adjuncts but never primary, and delaying adrenaline worsens outcomes. (Source: ASCIA Guidelines: Acute management of anaphylaxis)
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines: Acute management of anaphylaxis

options.text: Administer oral promethazine 10 mg and ranitidine 75 mg as combined H1 and H2 antihistamine therapy with reassessment after 20 minutes, plus consideration of a single dose of IM prednisolone 1 mg/kg as additional steroid
options.rationale: Antihistamines and steroids do not treat airway compromise or shock in anaphylaxis; adrenaline is the only first-line drug. (Source: ASCIA Guidelines: Acute management of anaphylaxis)
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines: Acute management of anaphylaxis

explanation.summary: Anaphylaxis is treated with IM adrenaline 1:1000 into the mid-anterolateral thigh as first-line therapy; doses are 0.1 mL (10 kg or less), 0.15 mL (10-20 kg, EpiPen Jr), 0.3 mL (20-50 kg, EpiPen) and 0.5 mL (over 50 kg). Repeat every 5 minutes if symptoms persist. Concurrent oxygen, supine positioning with legs elevated, and IV fluid resuscitation are essential. Salbutamol, antihistamines and corticosteroids are adjuncts only.
key_points: First-line: IM adrenaline 1:1000 mid-anterolateral thigh, repeat every 5 min
Doses by weight: 10 kg = 0.1 mL; 20 kg = 0.15 mL; over 20 kg = 0.3 mL; over 50 kg = 0.5 mL
Adjuncts (steroid, antihistamine, salbutamol) never replace adrenaline
explanation.pearls: Refractory anaphylaxis after 2-3 IM doses warrants IV adrenaline infusion under cardiac monitoring with anaesthetic/ED senior input; biphasic reactions occur in up to 20% and warrant observation.

sources.label: ASCIA Guidelines: Acute management of anaphylaxis

stem: An 8-month-old previously well infant is brought to ED in winter with 3 days of coryza, cough and worsening work of breathing. He has been feeding only one-third of normal and his last wet nappy was 6 hours ago. He has had two doses of low-flow oxygen at 1 L/min but remains tachypnoeic with subcostal recession. His parents have not yet given him any nebulised medications.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168, RR 68, SpO2 92% on 1 L/min nasal cannulae, T 38.0°C
data_table.Examination: Moderate subcostal and intercostal recession, bilateral wheeze and fine crackles, alert, capillary refill 2 s
data_table.Investigations: Nasopharyngeal swab: RSV positive; CXR not performed; cap gas pH 7.34 pCO2 48 mmHg HCO3 25

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in respiratory support?

options.text: Increase nasal cannula flow to 3 L/min and continue current observations with oral feeds
options.rationale: Standard low-flow cannulae above 2 L/min in infants is not better tolerated and does not provide positive pressure; HFNC is the next escalation. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Administer nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg every 20 minutes for three doses and reassess
options.rationale: Salbutamol does not benefit infants with bronchiolitis (under 12 months) and is not recommended in AU CPGs. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Give nebulised hypertonic saline 3 percent over 15 minutes and reassess work of breathing
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline has not shown consistent benefit in bronchiolitis and is not part of AU first-line management. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence intravenous dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg daily for 3 days and cease nasal oxygen
options.rationale: Corticosteroids do not improve outcomes in bronchiolitis and stopping oxygen at SpO2 92 percent risks deterioration. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence high-flow nasal cannula oxygen at 2 L/kg/min with continuous monitoring on ward
options.rationale: PARIS trial criteria are met (SpO2 under 92 percent on standard oxygen or persistent work of breathing); AU practice escalates to HFNC at 2 L/kg/min with reassessment at 1 hour. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis; PARIS trial)

explanation.summary: Bronchiolitis is a clinical diagnosis in infants under 12 months with viral coryzal prodrome progressing to wheeze, crackles and respiratory distress. AU first-line is supportive care with feeding support and minimal-handling oxygen for SpO2 under 92 percent. The PARIS trial established HFNC at 2 L/kg/min as an effective rescue once standard cannulae fail, halving CPAP/intubation rates. Salbutamol, steroids, antibiotics, hypertonic saline and chest physio are NOT recommended; they neither shorten admission nor reduce escalation.
key_points: Diagnose clinically; CXR rarely needed.
Oxygen for SpO2 under 92 percent; minimal handling.
HFNC 2 L/kg/min for failure of standard oxygen (PARIS).
Salbutamol, steroids, antibiotics, hypertonic saline NOT routine.
Watch for apnoea in babies under 6 weeks or ex-preterm.
explanation.pearls: Failure of HFNC at 1 hour (HR not falling, RR not falling, SpO2 not improving) is a strong predictor of CPAP escalation - assess all three trajectories at the 60 minute mark rather than relying on absolute values alone.

subtopic_detail: Moderate bronchiolitis HFNC and feeding strategy in infant
stem:
You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 4-month-old previously well term infant on day 3 of a coryzal illness now presenting with increased work of breathing and reduced feeds. He has had no apnoeas, is taking around 40% of his usual feed volumes, has had wet nappies 4 hours ago, and his older sibling is unwell with a viral upper respiratory tract infection.

He has no past wheeze, no atopy, no congenital heart disease and was not premature. Nasopharyngeal swab is pending. Trial of suctioning has provided only transient improvement and he remains in moderate respiratory distress on room air after observation in the ward over the last hour.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6, HR 168, RR 64, SpO2 89% room air, BP 86/52
data_table.Examination: Subcostal and intercostal recession, nasal flaring, scattered fine inspiratory crackles bilaterally, occasional expiratory wheeze, alert but irritable, capillary refill 2 seconds
data_table.Investigations: Nasal swab pending; VBG pH 7.34 pCO2 50 lactate 1.4; no chest X-ray done

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Trial nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg back-to-back and reassess in 20 minutes; if no improvement, give a single dose of oral prednisolone and admit for observation on the paediatric ward overnight
options.rationale: Salbutamol and corticosteroid have no role in viral bronchiolitis in infants under 12 months; routine use risks adverse effects without changing the disease course. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence humidified high-flow nasal cannula oxygen at 2 L/kg/min, place a nasogastric tube for continuous feeds at two-thirds maintenance and admit under paediatrics with hourly respiratory observations and senior review
options.rationale: Moderate bronchiolitis with hypoxia and feeding inadequacy is managed with HFNC and NG hydration at two-thirds maintenance, escalating if work of breathing or oxygen requirement worsens. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Administer intravenous ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg empirically for possible bacterial co-infection, take blood cultures, perform a chest X-ray and observe overnight on the paediatric ward with low-flow nasal prong oxygen
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics are not indicated for typical viral bronchiolitis without features of bacterial pneumonia, and routine chest X-ray is discouraged. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Intubate and ventilate the infant urgently in the resuscitation cubicle for impending respiratory failure given the elevated pCO2 of 50 on the venous blood gas and the persistent recession despite suctioning
options.rationale: A pCO2 of 50 on a venous gas in a settled infant with bronchiolitis is not an automatic intubation criterion; HFNC trial is appropriate first-line escalation. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Discharge home with parent education on suctioning, paracetamol for fever, advice to feed small amounts frequently and clear return precautions for apnoeas, marked recession or feeding less than half of usual volume
options.rationale: Discharge is inappropriate with SpO2 89% on room air and inadequate oral intake; admission with respiratory support is required. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Moderate bronchiolitis in an infant under 12 months with persistent hypoxia and inadequate feeding requires admission for respiratory support and hydration. Australian practice uses humidified high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min as first-line escalation, with nasogastric feeds at two-thirds maintenance to balance hydration with risk of fluid overload and SIADH. Bronchodilators and corticosteroids do not alter the natural history of viral bronchiolitis in this age group. Routine antibiotics and chest X-ray are not indicated unless atypical features are present.
key_points: HFNC 2 L/kg/min is first-line escalation in moderate bronchiolitis
NG feeds at two-thirds maintenance balance hydration with SIADH risk
Salbutamol and steroids not recommended under 12 months
explanation.pearls: Escalate from HFNC to CPAP if rising pCO2, persistent apnoeas, or worsening recession; intubation is reserved for true respiratory failure not responding to non-invasive support.

stem: You are the emergency department resident assessing a 4-month-old boy brought in with a 3-day coryzal illness and worsening cough. He has taken only half his usual feeds for the last 12 hours and has not had a wet nappy for 8 hours. He was born at term with no comorbidities. His 3-year-old sister had a cold last week. He is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. No vaccines are overdue.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6 °C, HR 168, RR 62, SpO2 92% room air
data_table.Examination: Subcostal and intercostal recession, fine crackles bilaterally with wheeze, mild head bobbing, alert and tracking carers
data_table.Investigations: Nasopharyngeal swab pending; routine bloods and chest x-ray not indicated

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next management step?

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg via mask with reassessment of respiratory work in 20 minutes
options.rationale: Salbutamol does not improve outcomes in viral bronchiolitis in infants under 12 months and is not recommended in the Australian paediatric pathway. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg single dose with planned review in the department in 4 hours
options.rationale: Systemic corticosteroids do not shorten bronchiolitis or reduce admission length and are not recommended by Australian guidelines. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Admit to the paediatric ward for nasal high-flow oxygen and supported feeding via nasogastric tube
options.rationale: Sustained SpO2 92% with significant work of breathing, tachypnoea above 60 and feeding under 50% meet admission criteria; nasal high-flow improves oxygenation and reduces escalation. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Discharge home with written red flag advice and follow-up by GP within 24 hours
options.rationale: Hypoxia, severe work of breathing and feeding under half are explicit admission triggers; discharge risks overnight deterioration and apnoea. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence intravenous benzylpenicillin and ceftriaxone with admission for monitoring
options.rationale: There is no clinical evidence of bacterial co-infection and empirical antibiotics are not part of the bronchiolitis pathway. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Bronchiolitis is a clinical diagnosis in children under 12 months driven by viral lower respiratory tract infection. The Royal Children's Hospital guideline indicates ward admission for sustained SpO2 under 92%, severe work of breathing, feeding under 50% of normal, apnoea or social concerns. Nasal high-flow oxygen is the standard escalation when low-flow oxygen and feeding support are inadequate. Bronchodilators, corticosteroids and antibiotics are not part of the standard pathway.
key_points: SpO2 below 92% triggers oxygen support admission.
Feeding below half of normal is an admission criterion.
Nasal high-flow is the preferred escalation in moderate disease.
Bronchodilators, steroids and antibiotics are not routine.
explanation.pearls: RSV-positive infants under 3 months and those born before 32 weeks are at highest risk of apnoea; admit even if SpO2 appears adequate, because apnoea may be the first sign of severe disease in this group.

stem: A 9-month-old infant is brought to a regional emergency department by his parents with 3 days of coryza and cough, and 24 hours of increasing work of breathing. He has taken half his usual feeds today and had no apnoea. He was born at 39 weeks and has no comorbidities. The nearest paediatric retrieval team is 90 minutes away.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 37.6 degrees Celsius, HR 158, RR 62, SpO2 88% in room air, BP 92/58 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Moderate work of breathing with subcostal and intercostal recession, widespread fine crackles and expiratory wheeze, capillary refill 2 seconds, alert and consolable

lead_in: Which is the single best initial respiratory intervention?

options.text: Commence low-flow nasal cannula oxygen at 1 L/min and admit to the paediatric ward
options.rationale: Low-flow at 1 L/min is unlikely to lift SpO2 above 92% with this degree of work of breathing and feed reduction, and does not deliver the positive distending pressure that high-flow provides for moderate bronchiolitis. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min with FiO2 to target SpO2 92 to 96%
options.rationale: PARIS criteria support high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min for infants with bronchiolitis who have hypoxia or moderate work of breathing, and it is the standard ward escalation step in Australian paediatric units. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Administer nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg with oxygen and reassess in 20 minutes
options.rationale: Salbutamol is not recommended in bronchiolitis in infants under 12 months because the dominant pathology is small-airway plugging, not bronchospasm, and trials show no meaningful benefit. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Administer nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1 in 1000 with continuous monitoring
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline may transiently improve work of breathing but has no impact on admission rate or length of stay, and is not recommended as a routine escalation step in Australian bronchiolitis pathways. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Intubate, ventilate and arrange urgent retrieval to a paediatric intensive care unit
options.rationale: Intubation is reserved for failure of HFNC and CPAP, apnoea or impending respiratory failure, none of which are present here; pre-emptive intubation increases ventilator-associated complications. (Source: PCH ED Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Moderate bronchiolitis with hypoxia and reduced feeds is managed with high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min as first-line respiratory support in Australian paediatric units. Bronchodilators and nebulised adrenaline are not part of routine therapy. Intubation is reserved for failure of HFNC, CPAP or apnoea. Most infants stabilise on the ward; failure on HFNC at 2 L/kg/min triggers escalation to CPAP and PICU consultation.
key_points: HFNC 2 L/kg/min is first-line escalation in moderate bronchiolitis with SpO2 below 92%
Salbutamol and nebulised adrenaline are not routinely indicated under 12 months
Reassess work of breathing and SpO2 hourly; failure to improve at 1 hour triggers PICU contact
explanation.pearls: PARIS trial showed HFNC at 2 L/kg/min reduces treatment failure in moderate bronchiolitis compared with standard low-flow oxygen at 2 L/min.

subtopic_detail: Recurrent wheeze in toddler: ICS preventer threshold
stem: A 20-month-old boy presents for review after his third episode of wheeze in 6 months. Each episode was triggered by a viral upper respiratory infection and responded to salbutamol. Between episodes he is well with no nocturnal cough or exercise limitation. His mother has a diagnosis of asthma. He has no personal history of eczema or food allergy.

data_table.Vital signs: RR 28, HR 120, SpO2 98% on room air, T 36.8 C
data_table.Examination: Well-appearing, no wheeze currently, no eczema, growth on 50th centile for weight and length
data_table.Medications: Salbutamol MDI via spacer PRN during acute episodes

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence low-dose ICS via spacer for an 8-week trial period
options.rationale: Three or more episodes of viral-induced wheeze in 12 months with a positive modified Asthma Predictive Index (maternal asthma) meets the threshold for a low-dose ICS trial per Australian guidelines. An 8-week duration allows adequate response assessment. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Continue salbutamol PRN alone and reassess in another 3 months
options.rationale: Three episodes in 6 months with a positive API exceeds the threshold for ongoing PRN management alone. Delaying a preventer trial risks further exacerbations and hospital presentations. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Commence montelukast 4 mg nightly as first-line preventer
options.rationale: Montelukast is second-line to low-dose ICS in young children with recurrent wheeze. Australian guidelines recommend ICS trial first due to superior efficacy evidence. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Refer for skin prick allergy testing before starting therapy
options.rationale: Atopic status informs prognosis but does not need confirmation before initiating a preventer trial. The clinical history and positive API are sufficient to begin treatment. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Commence high-dose ICS via spacer for a rapid 4-week trial
options.rationale: High-dose ICS is reserved for acute flares or step-up after failed low-dose trial. Starting at high dose in mild recurrent wheeze carries unnecessary adrenal suppression risk without additional benefit. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia)

explanation.summary: Recurrent viral-induced wheeze in preschool children is common and poses a management challenge. The modified Asthma Predictive Index helps identify children likely to benefit from preventer therapy. Australian guidelines recommend a low-dose ICS trial when there are 3 or more wheezing episodes in 12 months with risk factors. The trial should last 8 weeks before assessing response, and ICS should be ceased if no clear benefit is demonstrated.
key_points: Modified API: 1 major (parental asthma, personal eczema) or 2 minor (allergic rhinitis, wheeze without colds, eosinophilia >4%)
Low-dose ICS via spacer with mask is first-line preventer for preschool recurrent wheeze
Trial duration of 8 weeks allows adequate assessment before deciding to continue or cease
Montelukast is second-line, reserved for children unable to use or failing ICS
explanation.pearls: A positive modified API in a preschool child with recurrent wheeze predicts a 77% chance of active asthma at school age, making an ICS trial appropriate even without formal spirometry or allergy testing.

subtopic_detail: Sickle cell disease: acute chest syndrome management
stem: A 10-year-old boy with homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS) presents with fever, pleuritic chest pain, and progressive dyspnoea over 24 hours. He was admitted 2 days ago with a vaso-occlusive crisis affecting his long bones. His baseline haemoglobin is 80 g/L. He is receiving IV morphine and maintenance fluids.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 125, RR 32, SpO2 88% on 6 L/min nasal prongs, T 39.1 C, BP 95/55
data_table.Investigations: Hb 62 g/L (dropped from 80), WCC 18.2 x10^9/L, reticulocytes 2%, CXR: new bilateral lower lobe infiltrates, ABG: pH 7.38, pO2 58, pCO2 34
data_table.Examination: Bilateral basal crackles, reduced air entry at both bases, tender chest wall, tachypnoeic and using accessory muscles

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Escalate antibiotics to IV piperacillin-tazobactam and review
options.rationale: Broad-spectrum antibiotics are important but do not address the rapidly falling Hb and hypoxia. The primary intervention needed is to reduce HbS percentage and improve oxygen delivery. Antibiotics should be given alongside the definitive treatment. (Source: HSANZ: Sickle Cell Disease Guidelines)

options.text: Commence high-flow nasal cannula and monitor oxygen saturations
options.rationale: Supplemental oxygen is important but does not address the underlying pathology of HbS polymerisation and progressive sickling. With dropping Hb, bilateral infiltrates, and hypoxia on 6 L, the child needs transfusion to reduce HbS concentration. (Source: HSANZ: Sickle Cell Disease Guidelines)

options.text: Administer IV dexamethasone to reduce pulmonary inflammation
options.rationale: Corticosteroids in acute chest syndrome are controversial. While some studies show reduced length of stay, rebound vaso-occlusive crises occur frequently after cessation. They are not recommended as primary therapy and do not substitute for transfusion. (Source: British Society for Haematology: Sickle Cell)
source_refs: British Society for Haematology: Sickle Cell

options.text: Perform an urgent whole blood exchange via femoral line access
options.rationale: Automated red cell exchange (erythrocytapheresis) is indicated for severe or rapidly deteriorating ACS, but simple top-up transfusion should be attempted first when Hb is significantly below baseline. Exchange transfusion is reserved for Hb >90 g/L where simple transfusion would cause hyperviscosity. (Source: HSANZ: Sickle Cell Disease Guidelines)

options.text: Urgent simple red cell transfusion to raise Hb and reduce HbS
options.rationale: With Hb dropped to 62 g/L (below baseline of 80), worsening hypoxia, and bilateral infiltrates, urgent simple transfusion is indicated. The target is Hb 90-100 g/L (not >100 to avoid hyperviscosity). Simple transfusion is appropriate as the Hb is well below baseline. Phenotyped, sickle-negative, crossmatched blood should be used. (Source: HSANZ: Sickle Cell Disease Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Acute chest syndrome is the leading cause of death in sickle cell disease. It is defined as a new pulmonary infiltrate on CXR with at least one of: fever, respiratory symptoms, or hypoxia. Management includes supplemental oxygen, antibiotics (covering atypical organisms), analgesia (avoiding over-sedation), incentive spirometry, and transfusion. Simple transfusion is indicated when Hb is below baseline; exchange transfusion is reserved for severe ACS with Hb near baseline or rapidly deteriorating gas exchange despite simple transfusion.
key_points: ACS diagnosis: new CXR infiltrate + fever or respiratory symptoms or hypoxia in sickle cell disease
Simple transfusion when Hb is below baseline; target Hb 90-100 g/L (never >100 g/L to avoid hyperviscosity)
Exchange transfusion for severe/deteriorating ACS or when Hb is near baseline (>90 g/L)
Antibiotics must cover atypical organisms (macrolide + beta-lactam)
explanation.pearls: In ACS, a low reticulocyte count (<2%) in the context of a falling Hb suggests aplastic crisis (parvovirus B19 co-infection) superimposed on ACS, which mandates urgent transfusion as the bone marrow is unable to compensate for the acute haemolysis.

sources.label: British Society for Haematology: Sickle Cell

subtopic_detail: Bronchiolitis failing high flow nasal cannula: escalation decision
stem: A 4-month-old former 38 week infant has been admitted with RSV bronchiolitis. She has been on humidified high flow nasal cannula oxygen 2 L/kg/min at FiO2 0.5 for the past 4 hours. She remains tachypnoeic with marked subcostal recessions, intermittent grunting, and rising work of breathing. Capillary blood gas shows pH 7.22, pCO2 70 mmHg, HCO3 25 mmol/L, lactate 1.4 mmol/L. She is becoming less responsive.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 178, RR 78 with intermittent apnoeas, SpO2 88% on HFNC FiO2 0.5, T 38.0
data_table.Examination: Marked subcostal and intercostal recession, intermittent grunting, head bobbing, drowsy, reduced air entry bilaterally
data_table.Investigations: Capillary gas pH 7.22 pCO2 70 mmHg HCO3 25 mmol/L lactate 1.4 mmol/L; CXR hyperinflation without focal consolidation; RSV PCR positive

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Escalate to paediatric intensive care for non-invasive ventilation and intubation readiness
options.rationale: Rising CO2, respiratory acidosis, exhaustion, and apnoea on maximal HFNC are indications for ICU escalation with CPAP or intubation in infant bronchiolitis. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Trial nebulised hypertonic saline 3% every 4 hours to improve mucus clearance and oxygenation
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline is not recommended for routine acute care in bronchiolitis and does not reverse impending respiratory failure. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Start oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg and inhaled salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer hourly for 3 hours
options.rationale: Salbutamol and corticosteroids are not effective in viral bronchiolitis in infants under 12 months. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Empirical intravenous ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily for secondary bacterial pneumonia coverage
options.rationale: Antibiotics are not routinely indicated in bronchiolitis without a focal consolidation or strong evidence of bacterial superinfection. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Continue current HFNC settings and observe for another 4 hours before considering escalation
options.rationale: Continued observation in the face of rising CO2 and exhaustion delays definitive ventilatory support and risks decompensation. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Bronchiolitis is the leading cause of infant hospital admission in Australia and is managed with supportive care including nasal suction, hydration, and supplemental oxygen. High-flow nasal cannula is used for moderate disease, with thresholds for escalation that include rising CO2, persistent hypoxia despite maximal HFNC, apnoea, or exhaustion. Continuous positive airway pressure or intubation are delivered in paediatric intensive care for failure to respond to HFNC. Bronchodilators, hypertonic saline, antibiotics, and corticosteroids are not recommended for routine bronchiolitis.
key_points: HFNC failure includes rising CO2, exhaustion, apnoea, or persistent hypoxia at maximum settings
CPAP and intubation are the next steps in paediatric intensive care
Bronchodilators and steroids are not effective in viral bronchiolitis under 12 months
explanation.pearls: An infant on maximal HFNC with rising CO2 above 60 mmHg, falling pH below 7.25, exhaustion, or recurrent apnoea needs ICU escalation rather than further HFNC titration.

stem: A 4-month-old infant born at term is brought to the regional emergency department on day 4 of a coryzal illness. Her parents report increased work of breathing overnight and reduced feeding (around half her usual intake) but no apnoeas or cyanotic episodes. Sister had a similar illness 2 weeks ago. She is alert and consolable.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 156, RR 58, SpO2 93% RA, T 37.8°C, capillary refill 2 s
data_table.Examination: Mild intercostal recession, scattered fine inspiratory crackles bilaterally, occasional expiratory wheeze, alert and pink in air
data_table.Investigations: Not yet performed; NPA pending; no recent vaccinations

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Discharge home with parent education and a planned GP review the next day
options.rationale: Feeding at half normal intake with SpO2 93 percent and increased work of breathing places her above mild category; she does not meet AU discharge criteria. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Start high flow nasal cannula oxygen at 2 L/kg/min and transfer to PICU at once
options.rationale: HFNC is indicated when standard oxygen fails or for severe bronchiolitis (apnoea, persistent SpO2 under 90 on low flow, severe recession); her presentation is moderate. (Source: PARIS trial 2018; PREDICT 2021)

options.text: Commence nebulised salbutamol every 4 hours and inhaled corticosteroid twice daily
options.rationale: Bronchodilators and corticosteroids do not reduce admission length, oxygen need, or duration in bronchiolitis under 12 months; AU and PREDICT guidance recommends against routine use. (Source: PREDICT bronchiolitis guideline 2021)

options.text: Admit for observation with minimal handling and oral or nasogastric feeds as tolerated
options.rationale: Moderate bronchiolitis (RR 50 to 70, mild recession, SpO2 90 to 94, reduced feeds) is treated with supportive care and minimal handling; oxygen is added only if SpO2 falls below 90 to 92 per local CPG. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis 2023)

options.text: Order chest x-ray and commence empirical intravenous benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin
options.rationale: Routine CXR and empirical antibiotics are not indicated in bronchiolitis without focal signs or septic features. (Source: PREDICT bronchiolitis guideline 2021)

explanation.summary: Bronchiolitis severity in infants under 12 months drives disposition. Moderate disease - RR around 50 to 70, mild to moderate recession, SpO2 90 to 94 percent, reduced feeding around 50 percent - warrants admission for supportive care with minimal handling and replaced feeds. Bronchodilators, steroids, antibiotics and routine imaging do not change outcomes. Escalation to oxygen, then HFNC, then CPAP follows worsening work of breathing, oxygen requirement under 90 to 92 percent, or apnoeas.
key_points: Severity = RR, work of breathing, SpO2, feeding, apnoeas, consciousness.
Moderate disease: admit, minimal handling, NG/IV feeds if oral intake under 50 percent.
Avoid bronchodilators, steroids and routine CXR/antibiotics.
HFNC for severe disease or rescue when standard oxygen fails.
Discharge: SpO2 >= 92 RA, feeding well, no recession, parents confident.
explanation.pearls: In bronchiolitis the most reliable predictor of deterioration is apnoea history under 2 months of age - any reported apnoea should trigger admission regardless of current observations.

subtopic_detail: Acute moderate asthma in school-age child
stem: A 7-year-old boy with a known history of asthma is brought to the emergency department by his mother with a 12-hour history of increasing wheeze and cough triggered by a viral illness. He is using his salbutamol puffer with a spacer every 2 hours at home without sustained relief. He has no peanut or food allergies. His last admission for asthma was 12 months ago.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 132, RR 30, SpO2 93% RA, T 37.4°C, peak flow 60 percent predicted
data_table.Examination: Mild accessory muscle use, audible expiratory wheeze bilaterally, speaks in short phrases, alert and cooperative
data_table.Investigations: Not indicated initially; CXR reserved for atypical features

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Give nebulised salbutamol 5 mg every 20 minutes for 3 doses, then reassess clinically
options.rationale: Pressurised metered-dose inhaler with spacer is at least as effective as nebuliser in children and avoids unnecessary aerosolisation; nebuliser is reserved for severe disease. (Source: National Asthma Council: Australian Asthma Handbook 2024)

options.text: Commence high flow nasal cannula oxygen at 2 L/kg/min and refer to PICU
options.rationale: HFNC is reserved for severe or life-threatening asthma with persistent hypoxia; this presentation is moderate. (Source: ANZICS guidelines)

options.text: Administer intravenous magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg in normal saline over 20 minutes
options.rationale: IV magnesium is reserved for severe asthma not responding to initial salbutamol burst therapy; first-line is salbutamol plus steroids. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook 2024)

options.text: Give salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer every 20 minutes for 1 hour and oral prednisolone
options.rationale: Moderate exacerbation (SpO2 90 to 94 percent, accessory muscle use, peak flow 50 to 75 percent) is managed with salbutamol via spacer (6 puffs under 6 years, 12 puffs over 6 years - but 6 puffs is standard AU paediatric dose) every 20 minutes for 1 hour plus oral prednisolone 1 to 2 mg/kg (max 50 mg). (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook 2024)

options.text: Increase his inhaled corticosteroid maintenance dose and discharge with parent action plan
options.rationale: Maintenance ICS escalation is for ongoing control; acute exacerbation requires bronchodilator burst plus oral steroids. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook 2024)

explanation.summary: Moderate asthma exacerbation in school-age children is treated with salbutamol via pressurised metered-dose inhaler and spacer (12 puffs over 6 years, 6 puffs under 6 years) every 20 minutes for the first hour, plus a single dose of oral prednisolone 1 to 2 mg/kg up to 50 mg, with reassessment after each dose. Spacer delivery is at least as effective as nebuliser, avoids aerosolisation, and reduces emergency drug usage. Severe disease (SpO2 under 90, silent chest, exhaustion) escalates to nebulised salbutamol with ipratropium and IV magnesium.
key_points: Mild = SpO2 over 94, mild wheeze, normal speech: salbutamol 4 to 6 puffs, observe.
Moderate = SpO2 90 to 94, accessory muscle use: salbutamol burst + oral pred.
Severe = silent chest, agitated, hypoxia: nebulised salbutamol-ipratropium, IV magnesium, ICU.
Discharge: stable for 1 to 2 hours, SpO2 over 94, salbutamol 3-hourly or less; action plan; oral pred 3 days.
Refer all severe exacerbations to paediatric review within 1 week.
explanation.pearls: For children with frequent exacerbations or poor symptom control on SABA, consider starting low-dose ICS (budesonide 200 mcg twice daily) and the AsthmaSCAN action plan for written symptom tracking.

subtopic_detail: Preschool viral-induced wheeze first-line bronchodilator and steroid evidence
stem: You are the emergency department paediatric registrar reviewing an 18-month-old boy with the third episode in 6 months of wheeze, cough and increased work of breathing during a coryzal illness. He responded to salbutamol via spacer at home this morning but has worsened over the day with persistent wheeze and tachypnoea. His first wheeze episode was at 8 months. There is no atopic history (no eczema, no food allergy), no parental asthma, and no inhaled smoke exposure. He has met all developmental milestones and growth is on the 50th centile.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.4 °C, HR 152, RR 48, SpO2 92% room air, BP not routine
