data_table.Examination: Moderate work of breathing with subcostal recession, audible expiratory wheeze, prolonged expiratory phase, air entry symmetrical with widespread polyphonic wheeze, alert, mildly distressed
data_table.Investigations: No bloods or chest X-ray indicated routinely; NPS not required

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in his management?

options.text: Administer back-to-back nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg every 20 minutes for 3 doses with high-flow oxygen, add nebulised ipratropium bromide 250 micrograms every 20 minutes and arrange admission for IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg every 6 hours
options.rationale: Salbutamol via spacer (6 puffs at this size) is preferred over nebuliser; oral prednisolone is preferred over IV in moderate exacerbation tolerating oral; routine ipratropium is reserved for severe disease. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia))
source_refs: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Discharge home with an oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 3 days, salbutamol via spacer 6 puffs every 4 hours as required, with safety-net advice and review at 24 hours by GP for ongoing management of the acute episode
options.rationale: SpO2 92% with moderate work of breathing requires in-department observation with bronchodilator and oxygen titration before considering discharge; not an immediate discharge candidate. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia))
source_refs: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Administer salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer every 20 minutes for 3 doses with oxygen to maintain SpO2 above 94%, give oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg as a single dose, and reassess after the third salbutamol round before deciding admission
options.rationale: Moderate preschool wheeze responds to salbutamol via spacer (6 puffs) every 20 minutes for 3 doses; oral prednisolone in modest episodes in children with a clear viral-induced wheeze pattern who present to ED is now supported in Australian guidance. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia))
source_refs: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Commence inhaled fluticasone propionate 100 micrograms twice daily as a preventer from today given the recurrent wheeze episodes and review symptom control in 4 weeks before adjusting therapy or stepping up to combination inhaler
options.rationale: ICS preventer in pure viral-induced preschool wheeze without interval symptoms or atopy has limited benefit; initiation should be considered after 3+ episodes when guided by phenotype, and not as the acute ED intervention. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia))
source_refs: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Administer nebulised adrenaline 1:1000 5 mL with oxygen as is given for moderate-severe croup, on the basis that the wheeze could reflect upper airway involvement that has been mislabelled as lower airway disease
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline is for stridor in croup, not for lower-airway wheeze; this is not appropriate for viral-induced wheeze. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia))
source_refs: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

explanation.summary: Preschool wheeze (under 5 years) is heterogeneous - episodic viral-induced wheeze (no interval symptoms, no atopy) versus multi-trigger wheeze (likely asthma trajectory, often atopic). Acute moderate exacerbation: salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer (12 puffs for over 6 years) every 20 minutes for 3 doses with oxygen titrated to keep SpO2 over 94%. Oral prednisolone (1-2 mg/kg up to 50 mg) for moderate/severe exacerbations is recommended in Australian guidance even for preschool age presenting to ED. Ipratropium adds benefit in severe/life-threatening. ICS preventer use in pure viral-induced wheeze is debated; consider after 3+ episodes in a year, family history of asthma, or interval symptoms. Spacer technique is critical.
key_points: Acute moderate preschool wheeze: salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer q20min x3
Oral prednisolone 1-2 mg/kg (max 50 mg) for moderate/severe exacerbation
Ipratropium: add for severe/life-threatening
Spacer with mask under 4 years, mouthpiece from 4-5 years
ICS preventer: consider after 3+ episodes, atopy or interval symptoms
explanation.pearls: An infant under 12 months with a first wheeze illness is bronchiolitis not asthma until proven otherwise; reaching for salbutamol there is not evidence-based, but in a toddler with recurrent wheeze responding to bronchodilator, the phenotype tips toward asthma even before formal diagnosis.

sources.label: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

subtopic_detail: Bronchiolitis admission criteria and feeding support
stem: You are the paediatric ED registrar reviewing a 6-week-old term infant in winter with 3 days of coryza and increasing work of breathing. Mum reports feeds have dropped from 100 to 40 mL every 3 hours over the last 24 hours, and the baby is becoming sweaty during feeds. There is no apnoea history. Older sibling has a viral URTI.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6, HR 168, RR 64, SpO2 91% RA (rises to 95% on 0.5 L/min nasal prong), BP 78/46
data_table.Examination: Subcostal and intercostal recession; tracheal tug; bilateral fine inspiratory crackles and wheeze; capillary refill 2 s; mucous membranes slightly dry
data_table.Investigations: Nasopharyngeal aspirate RSV positive; no bloods or chest x-ray; weight 4.6 kg (50th centile)

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Trial of nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg every 20 minutes for 3 doses; oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg single dose; review at 1 hour
options.rationale: Bronchodilators and corticosteroids have no proven benefit in classic viral bronchiolitis under 12 months and are not recommended; supportive feeding and oxygen are the mainstays. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Admit; commence low-flow nasal prong oxygen to maintain SpO2 92 percent or above; nasogastric feeds at two thirds maintenance
options.rationale: Sustained SpO2 below 92 percent on room air, feeding less than half normal volume, and increased work of breathing meet admission criteria; nasogastric or intravenous hydration plus titrated oxygen is the supportive standard. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Discharge home with safety-net advice; review by general practitioner in 24 hours and red-flag information for parents
options.rationale: Hypoxia at rest below 92 percent, tachypnoea above 60 and reduced oral intake all argue against discharge; safety-net advice does not substitute for inpatient support. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Admit for high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min from arrival; nil by mouth and intravenous maintenance fluids
options.rationale: High-flow nasal cannula is reserved for failure of low-flow oxygen or persistent severe distress; routine first-line use does not change outcomes and overtreats. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Empirical intravenous benzylpenicillin 60 mg/kg and gentamicin 7 mg/kg; chest x-ray and full septic workup including lumbar puncture
options.rationale: RSV-positive bronchiolitis with no toxic features does not require sepsis workup or empirical antibiotics; secondary bacterial pneumonia would warrant focal findings and rising inflammatory markers. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Bronchiolitis is supportive care only. Admission criteria are SpO2 persistently below 92 percent on room air, feeds less than half normal volume, severe work of breathing, apnoea or social risk. Nasogastric tube feeds at two thirds maintenance are preferred over IV fluids when tolerated. High-flow nasal cannula is a rescue therapy when low-flow oxygen and feeding support fail to maintain saturations or normalise work of breathing.
key_points: Bronchiolitis is supportive: oxygen if SpO2 below 92 percent, feeding support if intake under half
Bronchodilators, steroids, antibiotics not routinely indicated
Nasogastric feeds at two thirds maintenance preferred to IV fluids
High-flow nasal cannula is rescue therapy, not first line
explanation.pearls: Apnoea risk is highest in infants under 6 weeks corrected age or with prematurity; admit for observation regardless of work of breathing.

stem: You are the paediatric ED registrar managing a 9-year-old girl with known asthma now in resus. She received three 12-puff burst-doses of salbutamol with spacer plus oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg 60 minutes ago. She remains in marked respiratory distress, with subcostal recession and accessory muscle use, only managing single-word sentences. She has had no recent viral illness; her last hospitalisation was 8 months ago. There is no fever.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8, HR 152, RR 38, SpO2 89% on 6 L/min mask, BP 108/68
data_table.Examination: Marked subcostal recession; accessory muscle use; minimal air entry; widespread expiratory wheeze; alert
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.34, pCO2 38, lactate 2.4; capillary glucose 6.8; no CXR yet
data_table.Medications: Salbutamol 12 puff x3 in 1 hour; oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg; ipratropium 250 mcg x3

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg (maximum 2 g) over 20 minutes; continue back-to-back nebulised salbutamol
options.rationale: After first-hour burst-dose salbutamol, ipratropium and systemic steroid have failed, IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg over 20 minutes is the next step in severe paediatric asthma before further escalation. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

options.text: Intravenous aminophylline loading dose 10 mg/kg over 1 hour; start maintenance aminophylline infusion immediately afterwards
options.rationale: Aminophylline is reserved for failure of magnesium and is used in ICU due to narrow therapeutic window; it is not the next step in standard escalation. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

options.text: Subcutaneous adrenaline 0.01 mg/kg (max 0.5 mg) of 1 in 1000; repeat at 15-minute intervals as required
options.rationale: Subcutaneous adrenaline is used for anaphylaxis, not pure asthma exacerbations; routine use risks tachyarrhythmia without addressing bronchospasm better than nebulised beta-agonist. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

options.text: Rapid sequence intubation with ketamine 1.5 mg/kg and rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg; transfer to paediatric intensive care
options.rationale: Intubation is reserved for exhaustion, hypercapnia or imminent arrest in severe asthma; the patient is still alert with normocapnia and IV magnesium has not been trialled. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

options.text: High-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min in 100% oxygen alone; cease bronchodilators and reassess in 30 minutes
options.rationale: Ceasing bronchodilators during active severe asthma is unsafe; high-flow nasal cannula is adjunctive support, not a substitute for pharmacological escalation. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

explanation.summary: Severe paediatric asthma escalation follows a stepped pathway: oxygen, burst-dose salbutamol with ipratropium and oral or IV steroid in the first hour. If severity persists, IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg over 20 minutes is the standard next step. Aminophylline and IV salbutamol are reserved for ICU-level escalation. Intubation is for exhaustion, hypercapnia or arrest; ketamine RSI minimises bronchoconstriction.
key_points: First hour: oxygen, burst salbutamol/ipratropium, systemic steroid
IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg is the standard second step
Aminophylline and IV salbutamol are ICU escalation
Intubation for exhaustion, rising pCO2 or arrest
explanation.pearls: A normal or rising pCO2 in severe asthma is a sign of failing respiratory effort and impending arrest; do not be reassured by a 'normal' venous gas.

subtopic_detail: Duct-dependent cyanotic heart disease - PGE1 initiation
stem: A 6-hour-old term neonate born in a rural hospital develops progressive cyanosis. The infant was well at birth with normal Apgar scores. Oxygen saturations were noted to be 72% on the routine newborn screen at 4 hours of age and have not improved with high-flow oxygen via nasal prongs. The infant is feeding poorly but is not in respiratory distress.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 155, RR 44, SpO2 72% right hand, 70% right foot on 2 L/min O2, Temp 36.6 C
data_table.Examination: Central cyanosis. No respiratory distress. No murmur audible. Femoral pulses palpable. Liver not enlarged. Active and alert.
data_table.Investigations: Hyperoxia test: SpO2 74% and PaO2 35 mmHg after 10 minutes on 100% oxygen via headbox.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Intubation with mechanical ventilation at high FiO2
options.rationale: This infant has no respiratory distress and the hypoxaemia is due to cardiac mixing or obstruction, not pulmonary disease. Mechanical ventilation with high FiO2 will not improve oxygenation in duct-dependent cyanotic heart disease and delays the critical intervention of maintaining ductal patency. (Source: RCH CPG: Cyanotic Heart Disease; Queensland Clinical Guideline: Neonatal Stabilisation)

options.text: Urgent echocardiography before commencing any treatment
options.rationale: While echocardiography is essential for definitive diagnosis, waiting for imaging delays treatment in a critically hypoxic neonate. A failed hyperoxia test in a well-perfused neonate without respiratory distress strongly suggests duct-dependent cyanotic heart disease, and prostaglandin should be commenced empirically before echocardiography. (Source: RCH CPG: Cyanotic Heart Disease)

options.text: IV fluid bolus and broad-spectrum antibiotics for sepsis
options.rationale: Neonatal sepsis can cause cyanosis but typically presents with respiratory distress, temperature instability, poor perfusion, and metabolic acidosis. This neonate is well-perfused with no respiratory distress and a failed hyperoxia test, which points to a fixed right-to-left cardiac shunt rather than infection. (Source: RCH CPG: Cyanotic Heart Disease)

options.text: IV prostaglandin E1 infusion and transfer to cardiac centre
options.rationale: A failed hyperoxia test (PaO2 remains below 100 mmHg on 100% FiO2) in a cyanosed neonate without respiratory distress is diagnostic of cyanotic congenital heart disease. Commencing prostaglandin E1 (alprostadil) at 5-10 nanograms/kg/min maintains ductal patency and is life-saving in duct-dependent lesions such as transposition of the great arteries, pulmonary atresia, and critical pulmonary stenosis. The infant should be transferred to a tertiary cardiac centre. (Source: RCH CPG: Cyanotic Heart Disease; Queensland Clinical Guideline: Neonatal Stabilisation)

options.text: Inhaled nitric oxide for persistent pulmonary hypertension
options.rationale: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn (PPHN) typically presents with labile saturations that improve then deteriorate, and often occurs in the context of meconium aspiration, sepsis, or perinatal asphyxia. A fixed cyanosis unresponsive to 100% oxygen with a failed hyperoxia test favours structural cyanotic heart disease over PPHN. (Source: RCH CPG: Cyanotic Heart Disease)

explanation.summary: Duct-dependent cyanotic congenital heart disease presents in the first days of life as the ductus arteriosus closes. The hyperoxia test is the critical bedside assessment: failure to raise PaO2 above 100 mmHg (or SpO2 above 85%) on 100% oxygen indicates a fixed right-to-left shunt. Prostaglandin E1 (alprostadil) reopens the ductus arteriosus and is life-saving. Common duct-dependent lesions include transposition of the great arteries, pulmonary atresia, tricuspid atresia, and critical pulmonary stenosis. Apnoea is the most common side effect of PGE1, so intubation equipment must be available.
key_points: Failed hyperoxia test (PaO2 below 100 mmHg on 100% O2) indicates cyanotic congenital heart disease
Prostaglandin E1 at 5-10 nanograms/kg/min maintains ductal patency and should be started empirically
Apnoea occurs in 10-12% of neonates on PGE1 - intubation equipment must be immediately available
Pre-ductal and post-ductal saturation difference greater than 3% suggests a ductal-level shunt
Echocardiography confirms the specific lesion but should not delay commencement of PGE1
explanation.pearls: Prostaglandin E1 causes apnoea in 10-12% of neonates - when commencing PGE1 at a rural or regional centre before retrieval, always have intubation equipment at the bedside and consider elective intubation before transport.

stem: A 5-month-old previously well infant is admitted with a 3-day history of coryza, cough, and progressively worsening work of breathing. She weighs 7 kg. Standard low-flow nasal oxygen at 2 L/min over the past 2 hours has been insufficient to keep saturations stable above 92 percent and feeds have stopped. She remains alert and the bedside nurse asks for your guidance on the next step. The respiratory consultant has been notified. She has no congenital heart disease.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168, RR 64, SpO2 90% on 2 L/min nasal prongs, Temp 37.6
data_table.Examination: Moderate intercostal and subcostal recession; tracheal tug; widespread expiratory wheeze with bilateral crackles; alert but tiring
data_table.Investigations: Clinical diagnosis; CXR not required routinely; nasopharyngeal swab pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence nebulised hypertonic saline 3 percent 4-hourly with 100 percent oxygen via Hudson mask
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline does not change duration of hospitalisation in bronchiolitis; rising oxygen requirement on low-flow nasal cannula indicates escalation to high-flow rather than nebuliser strategies. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Trial nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg back to back and reassess response after the third dose has been administered
options.rationale: Salbutamol has no demonstrated benefit in bronchiolitis under 12 months and is not recommended; the appropriate response is escalation of oxygen support. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Administer intramuscular adrenaline 10 micrograms/kg into the lateral thigh and prepare for emergent intubation now
options.rationale: Intramuscular adrenaline is reserved for anaphylaxis, not bronchiolitis; intubation may be needed later but is not the immediate next step before non-invasive escalation. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Start high-flow humidified nasal cannula oxygen at 2 L/kg/min FiO2 titrated to keep saturations above 92 percent
options.rationale: Heated humidified high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min is the recommended escalation when low-flow oxygen fails to maintain saturations above 92 percent in moderate bronchiolitis; the PARIS trial demonstrated reduced escalation to ICU compared with standard oxygen. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 3 days and continue current oxygen support without further escalation
options.rationale: Corticosteroids have no proven benefit in bronchiolitis under 12 months and should not be used routinely; rising oxygen requirement signals escalation. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Bronchiolitis in infants is managed primarily with supportive care: hydration via nasogastric or intravenous route as needed and oxygen titrated to maintain saturations above 92 percent. When low-flow oxygen is insufficient, heated humidified high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min reduces escalation to ICU compared with standard oxygen (PARIS trial). Salbutamol, steroids, hypertonic saline, and antibiotics are not routinely recommended.
key_points: Saturations target above 92 percent
HFNC 2 L/kg/min reduces ICU escalation (PARIS trial)
No role for salbutamol, steroids, antibiotics in typical bronchiolitis
Hydration: NG or IV when feeds dropping
explanation.pearls: PARIS trial: heated humidified HFNC at 2 L/kg/min reduces ICU escalation in infant bronchiolitis when low-flow oxygen fails; saturations target above 92 percent.

sources.label: PARIS trial - High-flow oxygen therapy in infants with bronchiolitis

stem:
An 8-month-old boy with known unrepaired tetralogy of Fallot presents to a regional ED with his mother. He is awaiting elective surgical repair scheduled in 6 weeks. This morning after a feed he became inconsolable, cried vigorously, and then turned a dusky blue colour with deep, rapid breathing. The episode has been going on for around 10 minutes by the time he reaches the resuscitation cubicle.

He has not vomited, has had no recent fever, and there is no diarrhoea or reduced wet nappies. Baseline SpO2 is usually 88 to 92% per cardiology letters. On arrival he is markedly cyanosed centrally with extremities mottled, breathing deeply at 70 per minute, and the previously loud harsh ejection systolic murmur at the upper left sternal edge is now barely audible. He has good IV access from the ambulance. His weight today is 7.8 kg and he has no known drug allergies.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 deg C, HR 188 bpm, RR 70 /min, SpO2 62% on 15 L/min via non-rebreather, BP 78/46 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Centrally cyanosed, irritable but rousable, deep Kussmaul-like respirations, soft 1/6 ESM ULSE (previously 4/6), CRT 4 seconds peripherally
data_table.Background: Known TOF, awaiting repair, baseline SpO2 88 to 92%, no prior tet spells documented

lead_in: After applying high-flow oxygen, which single next intervention is most appropriate?

options.text: Intravenous adenosine rapid push at the paediatric SVT dose followed by saline flush
options.rationale: Adenosine treats SVT, not a hypercyanotic spell; rhythm is sinus tachycardia from right-to-left shunting. Would be correct in narrow-complex SVT not responding to vagal manoeuvres (Source: RCH CPG: Tetralogy of Fallot).

options.text: Knee-chest positioning with IV morphine slow push
options.rationale: First-line management of a tet spell is knee-chest positioning to raise systemic vascular resistance plus IV or IM morphine 0.1 mg/kg to abolish hyperpnoea and reduce infundibular spasm, as per RCH CPG: Tetralogy of Fallot and AHA scientific statement.

options.text: IV frusemide to offload the right ventricle now
options.rationale: Frusemide reduces preload and worsens right-to-left shunting in a tet spell; volume expansion not depletion is needed. Would never be correct in a tet spell (Source: RCH CPG: ToF).

options.text: Commence prostaglandin E1 infusion peripherally
options.rationale: PGE1 maintains ductal patency in duct-dependent neonatal lesions; at 8 months the duct is closed. Would be correct in a neonate with duct-dependent cyanotic lesion (Source: BMJ Best Practice: ToF).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Tetralogy of Fallot

options.text: Intravenous sodium bicarbonate bolus before any other intervention
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is reserved for documented severe acidosis after positioning, oxygen, fluids and morphine have failed. Would be correct only as final step in refractory tet spell with confirmed acidosis (Source: AHA scientific statement).

explanation.summary: A tet spell is a sudden increase in right-to-left shunt through the VSD due to infundibular spasm or a drop in SVR. The murmur softens because pulmonary flow falls. Stepwise management: calm the child, knee-chest position, 100% oxygen, IV fluid bolus 10 to 20 mL/kg, IV or IM morphine 0.1 mg/kg, then IV propranolol 0.1 mg/kg or phenylephrine if refractory.
key_points: Murmur softens during spell (less pulmonary flow)
Knee-chest raises SVR, reducing right-to-left shunt
Morphine reduces hyperpnoea and infundibular spasm
Avoid agents that drop SVR or preload
Refractory: propranolol, phenylephrine, intubation, ECMO, urgent surgery
explanation.pearls: Beta-blockers are used both acutely and as a bridge to surgery to prevent spells; never give inotropes that increase contractility (worsens infundibular spasm).

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice: Tetralogy of Fallot

stem:
A 6-day-old term male infant is brought to a regional ED by his mother. He was born by uncomplicated normal vaginal delivery at 39+2 weeks with birthweight 3.4 kg, passed all routine newborn checks, and was discharged at 36 hours of life. Over the past 24 hours he has fed poorly, has been increasingly lethargic, and his mother noticed his legs looking mottled and feeling cool while his upper body remained warm.

He has had no fevers and no vomiting. On arrival he is pale, lethargic, and grunting. Brachial pulses are easily palpable bilaterally, but the femoral pulses are absent. Pre-ductal SpO2 on the right hand is 96%, while post-ductal SpO2 on the right foot is 84%. Blood pressure in the right arm is 78/44 mmHg and in the right leg is 42/28 mmHg, giving a clear upper-to-lower limb gradient. The liver is palpable 3 cm below the costal margin and there is metabolic acidosis on the venous blood gas.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.4 deg C, HR 180 bpm, RR 70 /min, BP arm 78/44 / leg 42/28 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Absent femoral pulses, brachial pulses present, mottled cool legs, warm upper body, hepatomegaly 3 cm, single loud S2
data_table.VBG: pH 7.18, lactate 6.8 mmol/L, HCO3 12 mmol/L, glucose 4.2 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management while awaiting transfer?

options.text: Intravenous frusemide and conservative fluid restriction
options.rationale: Diuresis is adjunctive for pulmonary congestion but does not address lethal ductal closure in duct-dependent systemic circulation. Would be correct only as adjunct once PGE1 has restored ductal flow (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Cardiology).

options.text: Intramuscular vitamin K 1 mg and oral feeds withheld pending surgical review
options.rationale: Vitamin K does not treat duct closure; PGE1 is required to reopen the duct. Would be correct as routine post-birth, not as immediate management of duct-dependent shock (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Coarctation).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Coarctation of the aorta

options.text: High-flow nasal cannula oxygen titrated to SpO2 above 95%
options.rationale: Supplemental O2 further constricts the ductus and accelerates collapse in duct-dependent systemic circulation; target SpO2 is 85-95 percent, not over 95. Would be correct only with confirmed non-cardiac hypoxaemia (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Intravenous adenosine rapid push to terminate suspected supraventricular tachycardia
options.rationale: No narrow-complex tachycardia described; differential pulses and saturation differential are diagnostic of coarctation. Would be correct in SVT (Source: RCH CPG: SVT).

options.text: Commence prostaglandin E1 (alprostadil) infusion via secure IV access
options.rationale: Duct-dependent systemic circulation from coarctation or interrupted aortic arch presents at day 5 to 10 with shock as the duct closes; PGE1 at 0.01 to 0.05 microg/kg/min reopens the duct and is the immediate life-saving therapy, per RCH CPG: Neonatal cardiology and BMJ Best Practice.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Coarctation of the aorta

explanation.summary: Critical coarctation presents at the end of the first week as the duct closes, producing shock, absent femoral pulses, upper-to-lower limb BP gradient, pre/post-ductal SpO2 differential, and metabolic acidosis. Immediate management is PGE1 infusion (commonly 0.01 to 0.05 microg/kg/min titrated), correction of acidosis, careful fluid resuscitation, and urgent transfer for surgical repair. Side effects of PGE1 include apnoea, fever, hypotension, and flushing; be prepared to intubate.
key_points: Duct-dependent systemic: coarctation, interrupted arch, critical AS, HLHS
PGE1 0.01 to 0.05 microg/kg/min IV
Apnoea is a common side effect; have airway ready
Avoid hyperoxia (constricts duct)
Definitive: surgical or catheter repair
explanation.pearls: If the duct closes before diagnosis, the infant can deteriorate from well to moribund within hours; any unwell neonate in the first 2 weeks needs femoral pulses and 4-limb BPs.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice: Coarctation of the aorta

stem: A 10-week-old infant born at 32 weeks gestation (corrected age 2 weeks) presents in July with 2 days of coryza and increasing feeding difficulty. She was discharged from the neonatal unit at 36 weeks corrected without home oxygen. She did not receive palivizumab. Her mother reports she managed only half her usual breast milk volume at the last 2 feeds and has had one brief apnoea witnessed at home.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 165 bpm, RR 62/min, SpO2 94% on room air, temperature 37.5 C, weight 3.1 kg
data_table.Examination: Mild subcostal recession, fine crackles right base, nasal flaring intermittently, alert between handling
data_table.Investigations: NPA: RSV positive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate level of care for this infant?

options.text: Discharge home with parental education and next-day GP follow-up
options.rationale: This infant has multiple high-risk features for severe bronchiolitis: prematurity (32 weeks), young corrected age (2 weeks), reduced feeding (below 50% of usual intake), and a witnessed apnoea. Discharge is inappropriate as the risk of deterioration is high. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis; Australasian Bronchiolitis Guideline)

options.text: Admit to a general paediatric ward for standard monitoring
options.rationale: While admission is appropriate, the combination of prematurity, very young corrected age, apnoea, and reduced feeding warrants a higher level of monitoring than a general paediatric ward can provide. Apnoea in the setting of bronchiolitis is an indication for continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring in a high-dependency or intensive care setting. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence HFNC at 2 L/kg/min and admit to a monitored area
options.rationale: HFNC is indicated when low-flow oxygen fails to maintain adequate oxygenation or there is moderate to severe work of breathing. This infant's SpO2 is 94% on room air with mild recession, so HFNC is not yet indicated. However, the high-risk features (apnoea, prematurity, feeding difficulty) mandate admission with continuous monitoring where HFNC can be rapidly escalated if needed. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Administer IM palivizumab and observe for 4 hours before deciding
options.rationale: Palivizumab is a prophylactic monoclonal antibody given monthly during RSV season to high-risk infants. It has no role in treating active RSV infection. Once the infant is symptomatic with confirmed RSV, palivizumab provides no therapeutic benefit. (Source: NCIRS Palivizumab Recommendations; RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Admit to a high-dependency area with continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring
options.rationale: This ex-premature infant with a corrected age of 2 weeks has confirmed RSV bronchiolitis with apnoea and reduced feeding. These features place her at high risk for apnoea and respiratory deterioration requiring escalation. Admission to a high-dependency or special care area with continuous cardiorespiratory and SpO2 monitoring is the appropriate level of care. Nasogastric feeds should be commenced for the reduced oral intake. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis; Australasian Bronchiolitis Guideline)

explanation.summary: Bronchiolitis risk stratification guides the level of care required. High-risk features include prematurity (especially under 32 weeks), age under 6 weeks at presentation, apnoea (including brief witnessed episodes), and reduced feeding below 50% of usual intake. Infants with apnoea in the setting of bronchiolitis require continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring as apnoea may recur unpredictably and can be fatal. Palivizumab is a prophylactic agent given monthly during RSV season and has no therapeutic role in active infection. HFNC is escalated when oxygen requirements increase, not pre-emptively based on risk factors alone.
key_points: Apnoea in bronchiolitis mandates continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring
High-risk features: prematurity, age under 6 weeks, apnoea, feeding under 50% usual
Palivizumab is prophylactic only and has no role in treating active RSV infection
HFNC is commenced when low-flow oxygen fails, not pre-emptively
explanation.pearls: Ex-premature infants born before 32 weeks gestation who develop RSV bronchiolitis in the first 6 months of life have a PICU admission rate of approximately 15%, compared with 2 to 3% in previously well term infants.

stem: A 3-year-old girl is brought to the GP within 10 minutes of eating a peanut butter biscuit at a friend's birthday party. She has known peanut allergy diagnosed at 18 months by RAST and has a current anaphylaxis action plan. Her mother saw her develop facial swelling and difficulty breathing in the car. She has had no previous anaphylactic episodes.

data_table.Examination: Generalised urticarial rash, swollen lips and tongue, audible inspiratory stridor, expiratory wheeze bilaterally, alert but distressed
data_table.Investigations: Not indicated acutely

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Administer intramuscular adrenaline 0.15 mg into the mid lateral thigh now
options.rationale: AU first-line for anaphylaxis in children 10 to 20 kg is intramuscular adrenaline 0.15 mg (150 mcg) into the mid lateral thigh; repeat every 5 minutes if needed. (Source: ASCIA anaphylaxis guidelines 2023; eTG)

options.text: Give oral promethazine 0.5 mg/kg and observe in the waiting room area
options.rationale: Oral antihistamines have no role in acute anaphylaxis and delay definitive treatment; IM adrenaline is first-line. (Source: ASCIA anaphylaxis guidelines 2023)

options.text: Commence intravenous hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg and arrange transfer by ambulance
options.rationale: Corticosteroids do not treat acute anaphylaxis; recent evidence shows no impact on biphasic reactions. (Source: ASCIA 2023)

options.text: Nebulise salbutamol 2.5 mg and oxygen 6 L/min via face mask before any other agent
options.rationale: Bronchodilators address asthma component but do not reverse upper airway oedema, hypotension or biphasic reactions; adrenaline first. (Source: ASCIA 2023)

options.text: Administer subcutaneous adrenaline 0.15 mg and call for paediatric emergency support
options.rationale: Subcutaneous adrenaline absorption is slower and unreliable in shock; the intramuscular route into the lateral thigh achieves peak plasma levels at 8 to 10 minutes. (Source: ASCIA 2023)

explanation.summary: Anaphylaxis is a severe systemic IgE-mediated reaction with airway, breathing or circulation involvement. AU first-line treatment is intramuscular adrenaline into the mid lateral thigh: 0.15 mg for 10 to 20 kg, 0.3 mg for over 20 kg, 0.5 mg for adults. The dose is repeated every 5 minutes as needed. Supportive care includes high flow oxygen, IV fluids for hypotension, and bronchodilators for bronchospasm. Antihistamines and corticosteroids are adjuncts only and have no role in acute reversal.
key_points: IM adrenaline 0.01 mg/kg up to 0.5 mg into the mid lateral thigh - first-line.
Repeat every 5 minutes if no response.
Lay patient flat (not sitting up) due to risk of empty ventricle syndrome.
Add IV crystalloid 20 mL/kg bolus for hypotension; nebulised salbutamol for bronchospasm.
Observe minimum 4 hours after symptom resolution; longer if biphasic risk factors.
explanation.pearls: Empty ventricle syndrome - sitting an anaphylaxis patient upright when hypotensive can precipitate cardiac arrest from inadequate venous return; always lay flat with legs elevated unless airway compromise prevents.

subtopic_detail: Supportive management thresholds in infant bronchiolitis
stem: A 10-month-old boy presents to your ED in late autumn with 3 days of coryza, rhinorrhoea, mild fever and 24 hours of wheezing and cough. His older sister had a cold last week. He has no prior wheeze, no atopy in the family, and is breastfed plus solid food. He is fully immunised. He has had no prior salbutamol use. He has 30 percent reduced feeding.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.8, HR 140, RR 50, SpO2 95 percent RA
data_table.Examination: Mild subcostal recession, widespread wheeze and bilateral fine crackles, mild dehydration with capillary refill 2 seconds; alert
data_table.Investigations: Nasopharyngeal aspirate pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Trial salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer with reassessment of respiratory effort and oxygen saturation at 20 minutes
options.rationale: Salbutamol is not effective in children under 12 months with bronchiolitis (no smooth muscle to act on, no benefit shown in trials); RCH recommends against routine use. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Supportive care with feeds and oxygen monitoring; admit if SpO2 below 92 percent on room air or feeds below 50 percent of normal
options.rationale: Bronchiolitis in infants is a supportive management diagnosis. RCH CPG recommends nasal saline, oxygen if SpO2 below 92 percent, NG or IV fluids if oral intake below 50 percent, and no routine salbutamol, steroids, adrenaline or antibiotics. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence high-flow nasal cannula oxygen at 2 L/kg/min as first-line therapy without trialling standard low-flow oxygen
options.rationale: HFNC is escalation for moderate-severe distress not maintained on standard low-flow; not routine first-line. (Source: RCH CPG)

options.text: Administer oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 3 days to reduce airway inflammation and shorten time to discharge
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are not effective in bronchiolitis (no benefit shown in multiple large RCTs). (Source: RCH CPG; Cochrane)

options.text: Administer nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 strength with continuous monitoring for 4 hours before reassessment
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline shows no benefit in routine bronchiolitis and may cause rebound; reserved for severe cases with failure of supportive measures. (Source: RCH CPG)

explanation.summary: Bronchiolitis is a clinical diagnosis in children under 12 months with viral lower respiratory tract symptoms. RSV is the commonest cause (60-70 percent). Management is supportive: gentle nasal saline, minimal handling, oxygen if SpO2 below 92 percent, fluids via NG or IV if oral intake below 50 percent normal, and apnoea monitoring in younger infants. Salbutamol, corticosteroids, adrenaline, hypertonic saline, and antibiotics are NOT routinely indicated. Escalation criteria: persistent hypoxia despite oxygen, exhaustion, recurrent apnoeas, severe distress.
key_points: Supportive care only - oxygen, feeds, observation
No salbutamol, no steroids, no adrenaline routinely
Admission if SpO2 below 92, feeds below 50 percent, apnoeas, severe distress
Antibiotics only if secondary bacterial infection
explanation.pearls: Apnoeas occur most commonly in infants under 6 weeks or preterm with bronchiolitis and may be the presenting feature even before respiratory distress is severe; cardiopulmonary monitoring is mandatory in this group regardless of initial SpO2.

stem:
A 3-month-old ex-term breastfed boy is brought to the GP for intermittent noisy breathing since 4 weeks of age that has slowly become more prominent. The noise is a soft inspiratory stridor that is worse when feeding, crying or lying supine, and almost resolves when held upright or in prone. He is thriving on the 50th centile, has had no fevers, no coryza, no preceding URTI, and feeds well with no choking or coughing.

On examination he is alert, T 36.8 deg C, RR 36, HR 130, SpO2 98% on room air, with no recession at rest. Auscultation is clear. There is no barking cough and no hoarse cry. Examination of the oropharynx is normal, the cry is normal in pitch, and there is no haemangioma over the beard distribution. He has not had any feeding-related choking episodes and there is no history suggestive of an inhaled foreign body.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C, RR 36, HR 130, SpO2 98% on room air.
data_table.Examination: Soft inspiratory stridor when supine, resolves prone or upright. No recession. Clear auscultation. Thriving on 50th centile.
data_table.Investigations: No fever, no preceding URTI. No cough, no choking with feeds.

lead_in: Which combination of most likely diagnosis and need for further investigation is correct?

options.text: Laryngomalacia; no investigation required while thriving without red flags (no cyanotic spells, feeding failure or failure to thrive); paediatric ENT referral only if red flags emerge
options.rationale: Positional inspiratory stridor starting in the first weeks, worse supine and with feeding and improving prone or upright in a thriving infant without red flags is the classic laryngomalacia picture, the most common cause of infant stridor. Australian practice is observation in the absence of red flags; flexible nasendoscopy and ENT referral are reserved for failure to thrive, severe feeding difficulty, cyanotic spells, or biphasic/atypical stridor, per RCH CPG: Stridor in children.

options.text: Subglottic haemangioma; arrange urgent MRI airway given biphasic stridor that worsens supine, with propranolol commenced after confirmation of diagnosis
options.rationale: Subglottic haemangioma gives biphasic (not purely inspiratory) stridor and often a beard-distribution cutaneous haemangioma; classically worsens with crying not posture. Would be the answer with biphasic stridor and visible facial haemangioma (Source: RCH CPG: Stridor).

options.text: Viral croup; commence oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg single stat dose and observe for 4 hours before considering discharge home
options.rationale: Croup is acute, with barking cough, hoarse cry, fever and age 6 months to 3 years; not chronic stridor from the neonatal period. Would be correct with acute onset and barking cough (Source: RCH CPG: Croup).

options.text: Inhaled foreign body; arrange urgent rigid bronchoscopy under general anaesthesia in theatre with combined ENT and paediatric anaesthetics involvement
options.rationale: Inhaled foreign body presents acutely with a witnessed choking episode and focal asymmetry; chronic positional stridor since birth excludes it. Would be correct with sudden-onset cough and unilateral wheeze in a toddler (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Vascular ring (double aortic arch or pulmonary sling); arrange CT angiography of the chest given the positional stridor pattern described in this infant
options.rationale: Vascular rings give biphasic stridor with feeding difficulty and reflux, often worse with neck extension, not improvement with prone positioning. Would be correct with feeding difficulty and stridor worse extending the neck (Source: RCH CPG: Stridor).

explanation.summary: Laryngomalacia is the most common cause of stridor in infants. Soft, supraglottic tissue collapses inward on inspiration, producing positional inspiratory stridor worst when supine, feeding or crying, and improving when prone or upright. Symptoms usually appear in the first 2 to 4 weeks of life, peak around 4 to 8 months, and resolve by 12 to 24 months in the majority. Severe cases (failure to thrive, apnoea, severe distress) need ENT referral and possible supraglottoplasty.
key_points: Most common cause of stridor in infants
Positional: worse supine, better prone or upright
Symptoms begin in first weeks, resolve by 12-24 months
Refer if failure to thrive, apnoea, severe distress, cyanosis
Diagnosed clinically; flexible nasoendoscopy confirms
explanation.pearls: If the stridor is positional, gradual onset, with a thriving baby and no fever, it is laryngomalacia until proven otherwise. Acute febrile stridor with a barking cough is croup.

stem:
A 5-week-old ex-34-week infant is brought to the resuscitation bay at a tertiary paediatric ED with day 4 of an RSV-positive bronchiolitis. Over the last hour she has had two witnessed apnoeic pauses of 20 seconds each with bradycardia to 80 and desaturation to 78% requiring tactile stimulation to recover. On arrival she has RR 70, HR 184, SpO2 89% on 2 L/min nasal prong oxygen, T 37.3 deg C.

She has marked subcostal and intercostal recession with head bobbing, nasal flaring and grunting. Air entry is fair bilaterally with widespread fine crackles and wheeze. She is mottled and her capillary refill is 3 seconds peripherally. She is lethargic but rousable. A venous gas shows pH 7.28, pCO2 65 mmHg, HCO3 24 mmol/L, lactate 2.1 mmol/L. NG feeds were started 12 hours ago and have been tolerated at 50% volume. The PICU registrar is at the bedside. She is fully immunised and weighs 3.6 kg.

data_table.Vital signs: RR 70, HR 184, SpO2 89% on 2 L O2, T 37.3 deg C.
data_table.Examination: Marked recession, head bobbing, grunting. Mottled, CRT 3 sec. Lethargic but rousable.
data_table.Investigations: VBG: pH 7.28, pCO2 65 mmHg, HCO3 24, lactate 2.1. RSV PCR positive. Two apnoeas with bradycardia in last hour.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate next step in respiratory support?

options.text: Increase nasal prong O2 to 4 L/min and continue NG feeds
options.rationale: Low-flow O2 does not unload respiratory muscles or prevent apnoea; nasal prongs cap out at sub-therapeutic support for type 2 failure. Would be appropriate only with mild work of breathing and SpO2 needs alone (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis).

options.text: Commence nebulised hypertonic saline 3% 4 mL six-hourly with nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline and salbutamol are not recommended in routine bronchiolitis in AU guidelines; they distract from definitive escalation. Would be considered only in older toddlers with viral-induced wheeze (Source: PREDICT Bronchiolitis Guideline).

options.text: Start high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min, titrate FiO2 to SpO2 92-96%
options.rationale: HFNC at 2 L/kg/min is the first escalation step for severe bronchiolitis with respiratory failure or apnoea, supported by the PARIS trial and RCH CPG. CPAP or intubation is reserved for ongoing apnoea, rising CO2 or exhaustion despite optimal HFNC. With recurrent apnoea and a pCO2 of 65 mmHg, HFNC is initiated now with clear escalation criteria (RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis; Franklin PARIS NEJM 2018).

options.text: Intubate immediately with rapid sequence induction and start mechanical ventilation now
options.rationale: Intubation is reserved for failure of non-invasive support (HFNC/CPAP), persistent apnoea, exhaustion or progressive hypercapnia despite optimisation. Premature intubation increases ventilator-associated complications. Would be correct after a failed HFNC trial (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis).

options.text: Administer IV dexamethasone and a single dose of IV ceftriaxone
options.rationale: Steroids and antibiotics are not indicated in uncomplicated viral bronchiolitis and do nothing for impending respiratory failure. Antibiotics only with proven bacterial co-infection or sepsis (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis; Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Severe bronchiolitis with apnoea, hypercapnia or persistent hypoxia despite low-flow O2 is treated with stepwise escalation: low-flow nasal prong O2 -> HFNC 2 L/kg/min -> CPAP -> intubation. HFNC is now the first non-invasive step (PARIS trial), reserving CPAP and intubation for failure. Antibiotics and salbutamol are not routinely indicated.
key_points: HFNC flow rate 2 L/kg/min, FiO2 titrated to SpO2 92-96%
Indications to escalate: ongoing apnoea, rising CO2, exhaustion, FiO2 >60%
CPAP useful for type 2 respiratory failure not responding to HFNC
Intubation for failure of non-invasive support
Salbutamol, steroids, antibiotics: not routine
explanation.pearls: Recurrent apnoea in a young infant is by itself a reason to escalate from low-flow O2 to HFNC or CPAP, even without severe hypoxia.

stem:
A 5-year-old boy presents to the GP after his mother noticed 4 days of cough, fever to 39.0 deg C, and lethargy. He is fully immunised. His older brother has a coryzal illness with low-grade fever. On examination he is flushed, T 38.7 deg C, RR 36, HR 128, SpO2 96% on room air. Auscultation reveals focal bronchial breathing and end-inspiratory crackles at the left upper zone with normal findings elsewhere.

He is mildly tachypnoeic but has no recession. CXR (obtained earlier at the request of an after-hours GP) shows a dense lobar consolidation of the left upper lobe with air bronchograms and no effusion. CRP is 124 mg/L, white cell count 18.2 x10^9/L with neutrophilia, and procalcitonin is 2.4 microg/L. He is feeding adequately and the GP needs to decide whether the picture supports bacterial or viral aetiology before prescribing.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.7 deg C, RR 36, HR 128, SpO2 96% room air.
data_table.Examination: Focal LUZ bronchial breathing and crackles. No recession. Otherwise normal.
data_table.Investigations: CXR: dense LUL lobar consolidation with air bronchograms. CRP 124 mg/L. WCC 18.2 with neutrophilia. Procalcitonin 2.4 microg/L.

lead_in: Which finding most strongly favours a bacterial (typically pneumococcal) aetiology over a viral cause?

options.text: Respiratory rate persistently around 36 breaths per minute today
options.rationale: Tachypnoea is a severity marker but non-specific; both viral and bacterial pneumonia produce it. Would never alone discriminate aetiology (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Childhood pneumonia).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Pneumonia in children

options.text: Dense lobar consolidation with air bronchograms on a focal CXR
options.rationale: Lobar consolidation supports bacterial cause but viral pneumonia (RSV, adenovirus, influenza) can produce lobar pattern, and bacterial can be patchy. Biomarkers are more specific. Would be more discriminating if combined with high procalcitonin (Source: RCH CPG: CAP).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Pneumonia in children

options.text: An isolated CRP of 124 mg/L without any other supporting features
options.rationale: CRP rises in both viral and bacterial disease; an isolated high CRP without neutrophilia or procalcitonin has poor specificity. Would be more discriminating combined with procalcitonin over 0.5 (Source: BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Pneumonia in children

options.text: Procalcitonin of 2.4 microg/L with neutrophil-predominant leucocytosis on the FBC
options.rationale: A procalcitonin above approximately 0.5 microg/L (and especially above 2) combined with neutrophilic leucocytosis is the most specific biomarker pattern for bacterial pneumonia; isolated CRP elevation and lobar appearance both have meaningful overlap with severe viral pneumonia. The combined pattern strengthens the bacterial diagnosis substantially (BMJ Best Practice; Stockmann 2018 J Pediatr).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Pneumonia in children

options.text: Symptom duration of 4 days, sibling with coryzal illness at home
options.rationale: Both bacterial and viral pneumonia commonly follow a viral URTI and sibling contact is non-discriminating. Would discriminate only with extrapulmonary atypical features (e.g. rash, haemolysis suggesting Mycoplasma) (Source: BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Pneumonia in children

explanation.summary: No single feature reliably distinguishes bacterial from viral paediatric pneumonia. The combination of procalcitonin elevation (especially >0.5 microg/L), neutrophilic leucocytosis, lobar consolidation on CXR and high fever is more specific than any single feature. In practice, all children with clinical pneumonia receive empirical antibiotics in Australia, but biomarker stratification informs duration and de-escalation.
key_points: Bacterial features: high fever, focal/lobar consolidation, neutrophilia, elevated PCT
Viral features: diffuse interstitial pattern, lymphocytosis, normal PCT, prominent URTI
Procalcitonin >0.5 microg/L is the best single biomarker for bacterial cause
Treat clinical pneumonia empirically; reserve narrow-spectrum oral amoxicillin first
Common pathogens by age: <2 viral, 2-5 viral plus pneumococcus, >5 add mycoplasma
explanation.pearls: Procalcitonin is more specific than CRP for bacterial infection in children but is not universally available; combined with neutrophilia and focal imaging it is the most powerful discriminator.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice: Pneumonia in children

stem: A 9-month-old previously well infant is brought to a regional ED with 3 days of coryza and 1 day of increasing cough and noisy breathing. He has not been feeding well today and his nappies are dry over the past 8 hours. He has had no apnoeas reported by his mother and is alert when stimulated. He is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander and is up to date with vaccinations. His chest auscultation reveals widespread fine crackles and mild expiratory wheeze.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 37.6 degrees Celsius, HR 158, RR 64, SpO2 90% on room air, capillary refill 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Moderate intercostal and subcostal recession, nasal flaring; cool peripheries; chest with widespread crackles and wheeze; abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: Point-of-care viral PCR positive for respiratory syncytial virus; capillary blood gas: pH 7.36, pCO2 42 mmHg, HCO3 22 mmol/L

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Admit, commence humidified low-flow nasal cannula oxygen to maintain SpO2 above 92 percent, support feeding with small frequent volumes or nasogastric feeds as needed, and reassess hourly
options.rationale: AU guidance targets SpO2 of at least 90 percent (not 92 percent); the higher threshold prolongs admission without outcome benefit and misstates AU policy. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Admit for trial of nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg every 20 minutes and intravenous hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg six hourly as the first-line therapy for moderate bronchiolitis in this child today
options.rationale: Bronchodilators and corticosteroids are not recommended in bronchiolitis under 12 months; supportive care is the mainstay. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Discharge home with parental safety-net advice, oral paracetamol 15 mg/kg six hourly and review with the GP in 24 hours if symptoms have not improved at all by then with rest
options.rationale: Hypoxia, dehydration and moderate work of breathing meet admission criteria; discharge home is unsafe. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence intravenous ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg once daily and order a chest X-ray as the first-line management for moderate bronchiolitis in this previously well 9-month-old infant today
options.rationale: Antibiotics and chest X-ray are not routinely indicated in straightforward viral bronchiolitis; supportive care is the mainstay. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Admit, commence humidified nasal cannula oxygen to keep SpO2 of at least 90 percent, support hydration with small frequent feeds or nasogastric top-ups, and reassess hourly
options.rationale: AU and RCH guidance for moderate bronchiolitis with hypoxia (SpO2 under 92 percent) and feeding difficulty is admission, oxygen to keep SpO2 of at least 90 percent, and feeding support; no routine antibiotics, bronchodilators or steroids. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Bronchiolitis is a clinical diagnosis in children under 12 months. Admission is indicated for SpO2 under 92 percent on room air, moderate or severe work of breathing, feeding under 50 percent of usual, dehydration, apnoeas or risk factors such as ex-prematurity. Treatment is supportive: oxygen to maintain SpO2 of at least 90 percent, small frequent feeds or NG hydration, and humidified high-flow if escalation is needed. Bronchodilators, corticosteroids, antibiotics and chest X-rays are not routine.
key_points: Admit if SpO2 under 92 percent or moderate-severe work of breathing or feeding under 50 percent of usual
Oxygen target is SpO2 of at least 90 percent, not higher, in stable bronchiolitis
No routine bronchodilators, corticosteroids, antibiotics or chest X-rays
explanation.pearls: Oxygen saturation target of at least 90 percent (not 92 percent) is the AU paediatric bronchiolitis admission threshold; aiming higher prolongs admission without outcome benefit.

subtopic_detail: Recurrent croup - investigation for structural cause
stem: An 18-month-old girl is brought to the emergency department with her fifth episode of croup in the past 12 months. Each episode has presented with barking cough, inspiratory stridor, and mild respiratory distress, and has responded promptly to a single dose of oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg. She has no history of endotracheal intubation or previous airway procedures. Between episodes she has been well with no stridor at rest, normal voice, and normal growth. She was born at term via uncomplicated delivery. She has no history of atopy or recurrent wheeze.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 128 bpm, RR 34/min, SpO2 97% in room air, temperature 38.1 degrees C, weight 11.2 kg
data_table.Examination: Intermittent barking cough, mild inspiratory stridor at rest, mild subcostal recession, no drooling or dysphagia, oropharynx normal, chest clear on auscultation bilaterally
data_table.Investigations: Previous CXR (3 months ago): normal subglottic region, no mass lesion, normal cardiac silhouette

lead_in: After treating this acute episode, what is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Continue dexamethasone as needed per episode
options.rationale: While each acute episode should be treated with dexamethasone, continuing to manage recurrent croup symptomatically without investigation is inappropriate after five or more episodes in 12 months. Recurrent croup raises concern for an underlying structural abnormality such as subglottic stenosis, haemangioma, or laryngeal cleft that warrants direct visualisation. (Source: RCH CPG - Croup)

options.text: Commence a trial of inhaled corticosteroids
options.rationale: Inhaled corticosteroids are not established therapy for recurrent croup and would not address a potential structural cause. While some evidence exists for reducing croup frequency, the primary concern with five episodes in 12 months is excluding an anatomical abnormality rather than empirically suppressing airway inflammation. (Source: eTG - Croup)

options.text: Arrange microlaryngobronchoscopy under GA
options.rationale: Recurrent croup defined as three or more episodes per year warrants investigation for structural airway abnormalities. Microlaryngobronchoscopy under general anaesthesia provides direct visualisation of the supraglottic, glottic, and subglottic airway and is the gold standard investigation. It can identify subglottic stenosis, subglottic haemangioma, laryngeal cleft, or other lesions predisposing to recurrent symptoms. (Source: RCH CPG - Croup)

options.text: Request a computed tomography scan of the chest
options.rationale: CT chest is not the first-line investigation for recurrent croup. It provides limited assessment of dynamic airway abnormalities and involves significant radiation exposure in a young child. The subglottic region and larynx are better evaluated by direct visualisation via microlaryngobronchoscopy, which also allows therapeutic intervention if a lesion is found. (Source: RCH CPG - Croup)

options.text: Refer for comprehensive skin prick allergy testing
options.rationale: Allergy testing is not indicated in this clinical scenario. The child has no history of atopy, recurrent wheeze, or allergic features. Recurrent croup is more likely due to structural airway factors or viral susceptibility than allergic disease. The priority is excluding anatomical abnormalities through direct airway assessment. (Source: ASCIA - Allergy Testing)

explanation.summary: Recurrent croup, typically defined as three or more episodes per year, warrants investigation for underlying structural airway pathology. Although each episode may respond to standard treatment with dexamethasone, the frequency of recurrence raises concern for conditions such as subglottic stenosis, subglottic haemangioma, or laryngeal cleft. Microlaryngobronchoscopy under general anaesthesia is the gold standard investigation as it allows direct visualisation of the airway and potential therapeutic intervention. A normal CXR does not exclude subglottic pathology.
key_points: Three or more episodes of croup per year warrants direct airway assessment
Microlaryngobronchoscopy under GA is the gold standard for evaluating structural airway abnormalities
Normal CXR does not exclude subglottic stenosis, haemangioma, or laryngeal cleft
explanation.pearls: Subglottic haemangioma typically presents before 6 months of age with progressive biphasic stridor and may be associated with cutaneous haemangiomas. Subglottic stenosis (congenital or acquired) is the most common structural finding in children with recurrent croup, often presenting with a subglottic diameter less than 4.0 mm in a term infant.
